question.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a donkey, or a human, or a monkey (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. No donkeys kicked Alfred. A monkey saw every donkey that saw it. Not all donkeys saw Hazel and every human saw Alfred. If Alfred doesn't see Lewis, then not all donkeys kicked Alfred only if no monkeys kicked Hazel.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. Alfred kicked every donkey.
2. Every donkey kicked Alfred.
3. A donkey kicked Alfred.
4. Not all donkeys kicked Alfred.
5. Alfred is a donkey.
6. Alfred kicked a donkey.

response.raw_response:
We translate the information into first-order logic (D = donkey, H = human, M = monkey, K(x,y) = x kicked y, S(x,y) = x saw y; A = Alfred, Ha = Hazel, L = Lewis).

1. Exclusive partition and existence  
   ∀x (D(x) ⊕ H(x) ⊕ M(x)) and ∃x D(x), ∃x H(x), ∃x M(x)

2. No donkey kicked Alfred  
   ∀x (D(x) → ¬K(x,A))               (†)

3. A monkey sees every donkey that sees it  
   ∀x∀y (M(x) ∧ D(y) ∧ S(y,x) → S(x,y))

4. Not all donkeys saw Hazel  
   ¬∀x (D(x) → S(x,Ha))

5. Every human saw Alfred  
   ∀x (H(x) → S(x,A))

6. Conditional about Alfred, Lewis, donkeys and monkeys  
   ¬S(A,L) → (¬∀x(D(x)→K(x,A)) → ∀x(M(x)→¬K(x,Ha)))

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Consequences

• From (†) and the fact that at least one donkey exists:

  – “Every donkey kicked Alfred” is false.  
  – Therefore “Not all donkeys kicked Alfred” is true.

Nothing else in the premises constrains who or what Alfred is, whom Alfred kicks, or whom the donkeys kick (aside from (†)).  Hence:

• “Alfred kicked every donkey”, “Alfred kicked a donkey”, and “Alfred is a donkey” can be made either true or false in different models that still satisfy all the given statements.

• “A donkey kicked Alfred” and “Every donkey kicked Alfred” are impossible (they contradict (†)), but the question asks for statements that must be true, not merely for those that are false.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Evaluation of the six candidate statements

1. Alfred kicked every donkey     – not forced (can be true or false).  
2. Every donkey kicked Alfred    – always false (so not “must be true”).  
3. A donkey kicked Alfred      – always false.  
4. Not all donkeys kicked Alfred   – always true (follows from (†)).  
5. Alfred is a donkey        – not forced.  
6. Alfred kicked a donkey      – not forced.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Final answer

The only statement that must be true under the given conditions is:

4. Not all donkeys kicked Alfred.

question.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a donkey, or a human, or a monkey (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. No donkeys kicked Hazel but it is not the case that Lewis didn't kick Alfred. Not all monkeys kicked Lewis but it is not the case that Lewis isn't a donkey. Lewis will run unless every donkey kicked Lewis. If every monkey saw Lewis, then not all humans kicked Hazel. If not all donkeys will run, then Lewis won't run only if not all donkeys kicked Hazel.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. Alfred and Lewis will run.
2. Alfred will run.
3. Hazel kicked Lewis.
4. Lewis kicked Hazel.
5. Lewis didn't kick Hazel.
6. Hazel didn't kick Lewis.

response.raw_response:
Premises (formalised)

1. ∀x [D(x) ∨ H(x) ∨ M(x)], exactly one of the three predicates is true of every x; ∃x D(x), ∃x H(x), ∃x M(x).

2. ∀x [D(x) → ¬K(x, Hazel)]      (no donkey kicked Hazel)

3. ¬¬K(Lewis, Alfred)            (Lewis did kick Alfred)

4. ¬∀x [M(x) → K(x, Lewis)]      (some monkey did not kick Lewis)

5. ¬¬D(Lewis)                    (Lewis is a donkey)

6. (¬∀x [D(x) → K(x, Lewis)]) → R(Lewis)
   (if there is a donkey that didn’t kick Lewis, Lewis will run)

7. (∀x [M(x) → S(x, Lewis)]) → ¬∀x [H(x) → K(x, Hazel)]

8. (¬∀x [D(x) → R(x)]) → (¬R(Lewis) → ¬∀x [D(x) → K(x, Hazel)])

Symbols:  D – donkey, H – human, M – monkey  
           K(a,b) – a kicked b  
           S(a,b) – a saw b  
           R(a)   – a will run

Deriving what must be true

• From (5) we have D(Lewis).  
• Premise (2) says every donkey did not kick Hazel. Combining with D(Lewis):

      D(Lewis) ∧ ∀x [D(x) → ¬K(x, Hazel)]
      ⟹ ¬K(Lewis, Hazel).

  So statement 5 (“Lewis didn't kick Hazel”) is entailed.

Checking the other candidate conclusions

We show that each of the remaining statements can fail in some model that satisfies all the premises, so none of them is forced.

Construct a consistent model:

  Individuals: Lewis (donkey), Donna (donkey), Hazel (human),
               Alfred (monkey).

  True atomic facts chosen so that all premises are met:
  – K(Lewis, Alfred) is true (premise 3).  
  – K(Donna, Hazel) and K(Lewis, Hazel) are false (premise 2).  
  – K(Alfred, Lewis) is false (so “some monkey didn’t kick Lewis”, premise 4).  
  – K(Donna, Lewis) and K(Lewis, Lewis) are true, so every donkey kicked Lewis; hence the antecedent of (6) is false, letting us set R(Lewis) to false without violating (6).  
  – Set R(Donna) = true, R(Lewis) = false; now “not all donkeys will run” is true, and because no donkey kicked Hazel, the consequent of (8) is satisfied.  
  – All other kicks, saws and runs can be arranged arbitrarily.

In this model:
  • R(Lewis) is false, so statements 1 and 2 (“Alfred and Lewis will run”, “Alfred will run”) can be made false (choose R(Alfred)=false).  
  • K(Hazel, Lewis) can be chosen either way, so statements 3 and 6 are not forced.  
  • K(Lewis, Hazel) is false (by premise 2), so statement 4 (“Lewis kicked Hazel”) is false.

Thus the only statement that must always be true is:

5. Lewis didn't kick Hazel.

All other listed statements are not logically forced by the premises.

Final answer: Only statement 5 must be true.

Consider the following situation:

Everything is a donkey, or a human, or a monkey (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. Alfred and Hazel are happy. Hazel doesn't see Hazel and a monkey kicked Alfred. If Lewis is a donkey, then every monkey is hungry only if every monkey saw Hazel.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. Lewis is happy.
2. Alfred is happy and Hazel doesn't see Hazel.
3. Alfred is happy but it is not the case that Hazel doesn't see Hazel.
4. Lewis and Alfred are happy.
5. Alfred isn't happy.
6. Alfred and Lewis are happy.

response.raw_response:
The information we are told is:

1. Alfred is happy.  
2. Hazel is happy.  
3. Hazel does not see Hazel.  
4. Some monkey kicked Alfred.  
5. If Lewis is a donkey, then  
   ( every monkey is hungry  ⇒  every monkey saw Hazel ).

Nothing else is fixed.

Now test each candidate statement against these facts.

1. “Lewis is happy.”  
   – Lewis’s happiness is never mentioned, so he might be happy or unhappy.  
   – Not forced.

2. “Alfred is happy and Hazel doesn’t see Hazel.”  
   – Both parts are explicitly given (1) and (3).  
   – Always true.  (Must be true.)

3. “Alfred is happy but Hazel DOES see Hazel.”  
   – Contradicts (3).  
   – Impossible; hence not forced true.

4. “Lewis and Alfred are happy.”  
   – Alfred is happy, but Lewis need not be.  
   – Not forced.

5. “Alfred isn’t happy.”  
   – Contradicts (1).  
   – Impossible; hence not forced true.

6. “Alfred and Lewis are happy.”  
   – Same problem as (4): Lewis’s happiness is undetermined.  
   – Not forced.

Therefore the only statement that must hold in every situation satisfying the premises is:

Statement 2:  “Alfred is happy and Hazel doesn’t see Hazel.”

question.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a donkey, or a human, or a monkey (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. Hazel or Lewis are hungry. If not all humans are asleep, then Hazel kicked Alfred only if every human saw Hazel. Only humans are hungry but it is not the case that Hazel isn't asleep.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. Not all humans are hungry.
2. Every human is hungry.
3. A human is hungry.
4. Alfred is asleep.
5. No humans are hungry.
6. Only humans are hungry and not all humans are hungry.

response.raw_response:
Let  

H(x) … “x is a human”  
Hungry(x) … “x is hungry”  
Asleep(x) … “x is asleep”

Given premises

1. Hungry(Hazel) ∨ Hungry(Lewis).                                     (Hazel-or-Lewis hungry)

2. ¬(∀x (H(x) → Asleep(x))) → (Kicked(Hazel,Alfred) → ∀x(H(x) → Saw(x,Hazel))).  
   (concerns kicking/seeing; irrelevant to hunger/asleep unless some human is awake)

3. ∀x (Hungry(x) → H(x)).                                             (Only humans are hungry)

4. Asleep(Hazel).                                                     (Hazel is asleep)

5. The individuals are exclusively donkeys, humans or monkeys, with at least one of each.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
What the premises force

• From (1) we know someone is hungry.  
• From (3) whoever is hungry must be a human.

Therefore there exists at least one hungry human:

∃x (H(x) ∧ Hungry(x)).

So statement 3 (“A human is hungry”) is true in every model that satisfies the premises.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Checking the remaining candidate conclusions

We only have to see whether the other statements can fail in some model that still satisfies every premise.

Build a model:

  – Objects: Hazel (donkey), Lewis (human), Mabel (monkey).  
  – Hungry: Lewis only.  
  – Asleep: Hazel (given); let Lewis be asleep as well.  
  – All other predicates can be arranged so that condition (2) is vacuously true, e.g. nobody is awake, so the antecedent of 2 is false.

This model fulfils every premise, yet

  • Every human is hungry (Lewis is the only human) → statement 2 true here.  
  • Not all humans are hungry → false here.  
  • Alfred is asleep → no constraint, assume false.  
  • No humans are hungry → false.  
  • “Only humans are hungry and not all humans are hungry” → false.

Because “Not all humans are hungry”, “Alfred is asleep”, “No humans are hungry”, and “Only humans are hungry and not all humans are hungry” can all be false while the premises hold, none of them is forced by the premises. Statement 2 can be true or false depending on how many humans we introduce (add another human who is not hungry and it becomes false), so it is not forced either.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Conclusion

The sole statement that must be true under the given information is

3. A human is hungry.

question.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a donkey, or a human, or a monkey (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. No humans saw Alfred. A human is happy just in case not all donkeys chased Alfred. A human saw Alfred just in case Hazel isn't hungry.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. Hazel is hungry.
2. Alfred isn't happy.
3. Alfred and Hazel are happy.
4. Alfred is happy.
5. Hazel isn't hungry.
6. Hazel is happy.

response.raw_response:
Step 1.  Translate the three English rules into ordinary logic  

U = set of all individuals; every member of U is either a Donkey, a Human or a Monkey (and there is at least one of each).

R1.  ∀x( Human(x) → ¬Saw(x, Alfred) )                       (No human saw Alfred)

R2.  ∀x( Human(x) → ( Happy(x) ↔ ¬∀y( Donkey(y) → Chased(y, Alfred) ) ) )
     (A human is happy ↔ not-all donkeys chased Alfred)

R3.  (∃x( Human(x) ∧ Saw(x, Alfred) ))  ↔  ¬Hungry(Hazel)
     (A human saw Alfred ↔ Hazel isn’t hungry)

“Just in case’’ is understood as a biconditional (↔).

Step 2.  Use R1 inside R3  

Because of R1 every human fails to see Alfred, so

    ∃x( Human(x) ∧ Saw(x, Alfred) )          is false.

Hence, for R3 to hold, the right–hand side of the biconditional must also be false:

    ¬Hungry(Hazel)   is false  ⇒  Hungry(Hazel) is true.

So Hazel is necessarily hungry.

Step 3.  Examine the remaining six candidate statements

1. Hazel is hungry.                     — proved true in every model.

2. Alfred isn’t happy.                  — not forced:
     make Alfred a donkey and simply stipulate Happy(Alfred) = true.

3. Alfred and Hazel are happy.          — not forced:
     in the same model, Hazel’s happiness can be set to false.

4. Alfred is happy.                     — not forced:
     make Alfred a donkey with Happy(Alfred) = false.

5. Hazel isn’t hungry.                  — contradicted by Step 2.

6. Hazel is happy.                      — not forced:
     e.g. let Hazel be a donkey and stipulate Happy(Hazel) = false,
     or let Hazel be a human while all donkeys did chase Alfred,
     which by R2 makes every human (including Hazel) unhappy.

Each of these alternative models respects every given rule as well as the
stipulation that there is at least one donkey, one human and one monkey.

Step 4.  Final conclusion  

The only statement that must be true in every situation satisfying the
premises is

1. Hazel is hungry.

All other listed statements may be true in some models and false in others, so none of them is logically forced.

question.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a donkey, or a human, or a monkey (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. Every monkey saw a human that saw it. Alfred drank unless every human saw Hazel. A monkey is hungry and Lewis isn't happy.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. A human saw every monkey.
2. Every human saw a monkey.
3. Every human saw every monkey.
4. No humans saw a monkey.
5. A human saw a monkey.
6. No humans saw every monkey.

response.raw_response:
Let  

– Donkey(x), Human(x), Monkey(x) partition the whole domain (each object is exactly one of these, and each sort is non-empty).  
– Saw(x,y) mean “x saw y”.  

Given fact  
F: ∀m (Monkey(m) → ∃h (Human(h) ∧ Saw(m,h) ∧ Saw(h,m)))  
  (Every monkey saw a human who in turn saw that monkey.)

From F we can immediately infer

• Because there is at least one monkey, choose such a monkey m₀.  
 F gives a human h₀ with Human(h₀) ∧ Saw(h₀,m₀).  
 Therefore: “A human saw a monkey” is always true.

Now test each candidate statement.

1. ∃h (Human(h) ∧ ∀m Monkey(m) → Saw(h,m))  
 Need not hold: with two monkeys m₁,m₂ and two humans h₁,h₂ where h₁ only sees m₁ and h₂ only sees m₂, F is satisfied but 1 is false.

2. ∀h (Human(h) → ∃m Monkey(m) ∧ Saw(h,m))  
 Need not hold: add a human who sees no monkey.

3. ∀h∀m (Human(h) ∧ Monkey(m) → Saw(h,m))  
 Even stronger than 2, so not forced.

4. ¬∃h,m (Human(h) ∧ Monkey(m) ∧ Saw(h,m))  
 Contradicts F (since some human always sees some monkey), so impossible.

5. ∃h,m (Human(h) ∧ Monkey(m) ∧ Saw(h,m))  
 As shown above, this follows directly from F; it must be true in every model.

6. ¬∃h (Human(h) ∧ ∀m Monkey(m) → Saw(h,m))  
 Fails in a model with only one monkey and one human who see each other, so not forced.

Hence the only statement that must always be true is:

5. A human saw a monkey.

question.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a donkey, or a human, or a monkey (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. Lewis isn't happy. Alfred and Lewis are hungry. No donkeys chased a monkey.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. Lewis isn't happy but it is not the case that Lewis isn't hungry.
2. Alfred and Lewis are happy.
3. Lewis isn't happy and Lewis isn't hungry.
4. Alfred isn't happy.
5. Alfred is happy.
6. Lewis is happy.

response.raw_response:
Premises that are explicitly given:

1. Every creature is exactly one of: donkey, human, or monkey, and there is at least one of each.
2. Lewis isn’t happy.                             (¬Happy(Lewis))
3. Alfred and Lewis are hungry.                  (Hungry(Alfred) ∧ Hungry(Lewis))
4. No donkey chased a monkey.                ∀x∀y [(Donkey(x) ∧ Monkey(y)) → ¬Chased(x,y)]

The six candidate conclusions:

1. Lewis isn’t happy but it is not the case that Lewis isn’t hungry.  
   (¬Happy(Lewis) ∧ ¬¬Hungry(Lewis))  ⇔  (¬Happy(Lewis) ∧ Hungry(Lewis))

2. Alfred and Lewis are happy.  
   (Happy(Alfred) ∧ Happy(Lewis))

3. Lewis isn’t happy and Lewis isn’t hungry.  
   (¬Happy(Lewis) ∧ ¬Hungry(Lewis))

4. Alfred isn’t happy.  
   (¬Happy(Alfred))

5. Alfred is happy.  
   (Happy(Alfred))

6. Lewis is happy.  
   (Happy(Lewis))

Logical consequences:

• From premise 2 we know Lewis is not happy.  
• From premise 3 we know Lewis is hungry (and Alfred is hungry).

Therefore:

– Statement 1 combines those two facts and is certainly true in every model of the premises.

– Statement 2 is impossible, because Happy(Lewis) is already known to be false.

– Statement 3 contradicts the fact that Lewis is hungry, hence cannot be true.

– Statements 4 and 5 concern Alfred’s happiness; we are told nothing about it, so neither of them is forced to be true.

– Statement 6 is directly contradicted by premise 2.

Hence the only statement that must be true is:

Statement 1.

Consider the following situation:

Everything is a donkey, or a human, or a monkey (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. Only humans are hungry and not all monkeys kicked Lewis. Hazel doesn't see Hazel but it is not the case that Lewis isn't a donkey. Alfred isn't a human unless no humans are hungry.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. Lewis is hungry.
2. Alfred is hungry.
3. Alfred isn't hungry.
4. Alfred and Hazel are hungry.
5. Alfred and Lewis are hungry.

response.raw_response:
First translate the information into precise logical statements.

Species (mutually exclusive and jointly exhaustive):  
D(x) – donkey H(x) – human M(x) – monkey  
Hungry(x) – ‘x is hungry’

Given facts  

A. Hungry(x) → H(x)      (only humans are hungry)  
B. ¬∀x (M(x) → kicked-Lewis(x))  (not every monkey kicked Lewis)  
C. ¬sees(Hazel , Hazel)  
D. D(Lewis)            (Lewis is a donkey)  
E. (∃x (H(x) ∧ Hungry(x))) → ¬H(Alfred)  
   (“Alfred isn’t a human unless no humans are hungry”  
    i.e. if some human is hungry then Alfred is not a human)

What the statements would mean

1. Lewis is hungry.  
2. Alfred is hungry.  
3. Alfred isn’t hungry.  
4. Alfred and Hazel are hungry.  
5. Alfred and Lewis are hungry.

Step-by-step consequences

1. From D(Lewis) and A (Hungry → Human, with species exclusive), Lewis cannot be hungry.  
   Therefore statement 1 is false in every model.

2. Suppose, for contradiction, that Alfred is hungry.  
   • By A, Alfred would have to be human.  
   • Thus ∃x (H(x) ∧ Hungry(x)) is true (take x = Alfred).  
   • But then E forces ¬H(Alfred).  
   • Contradiction (Alfred both human and not human).  

   Hence Alfred cannot be hungry in any model; Alfred is necessarily not hungry.

   Therefore  
   – statement 2 (“Alfred is hungry”) is impossible (always false);  
   – statement 3 (“Alfred isn’t hungry”) is inevitable (always true).

3. Since Alfred is never hungry, any conjunction that contains “Alfred is hungry”
   is never true.  Thus statements 4 and 5 are also impossible.

Conclusion

The only statement that must be true in every situation satisfying the conditions is:

3. Alfred isn’t hungry.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

3. Lewis ate.
4. Lewis isn't a human.
5. Hazel ate.
6. Lewis and Hazel ate.

response.raw_response:
Premises  
1. Every individual is exclusively a donkey, a human or a monkey, and there is at least one of each.  
2. No human chased every monkey.  
3. Lewis chased every monkey.  
4. If Hazel is not a human, then Hazel ate.   (¬Human(Hazel) → Ate(Hazel))

Deduction  
• From premise 2:  Human(x) → ¬Chased-every-monkey(x).  
  Its contrapositive is  Chased-every-monkey(x) → ¬Human(x).  

• Lewis chased every monkey (premise 3), therefore by the contrapositive above,  
  ¬Human(Lewis).  So Lewis is not a human.

Nothing in the premises links eating with chasing, or with being a donkey/monkey/human (except Hazel’s conditional).  
Hence:

– Lewis might eat or might not eat.  
– Hazel might be human (then the conditional is vacuously true whether she eats or not) or might be non-human (then she must eat).  So “Hazel ate’’ is not forced.  

Testing the six candidate conclusions:

1. Lewis is a human.    Not forced (in fact impossible).  
2. Lewis didn’t eat.    Not forced.  
3. Lewis ate.        Not forced.  
4. Lewis isn’t a human.  Must be true (proved above).  
5. Hazel ate.        Not forced.  
6. Lewis and Hazel ate.  Not forced.

Final answer:  
Only statement 4 (“Lewis isn’t a human”) must be true.

question.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a donkey, or a human, or a monkey (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. Not all monkeys kicked Lewis and Lewis is happy. Every monkey will run or Lewis is a donkey. Not all humans will run and Lewis doesn't see Hazel.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. A monkey kicked Lewis or not all monkeys saw Hazel.
2. Hazel doesn't see Hazel and every monkey kicked Lewis.
3. Not all monkeys kicked Lewis but it is not the case that Lewis doesn't see Hazel.
4. Not all monkeys kicked Lewis and Lewis doesn't see Hazel.
5. Hazel doesn't see Hazel and not all monkeys kicked Lewis.
6. Hazel saw Hazel and not all monkeys kicked Lewis.

response.raw_response:
We translate the given information into simple predicates.

Kinds (mutually exclusive, jointly exhaustive):  
D(x) – x is a donkey  
H(x) – x is a human  
M(x) – x is a monkey

Other predicates:  
K(x) – x kicked Lewis  
R(x) – x will run  
S(x , y) – x sees y  
L – Lewis  
Ha – Hazel  

Stated facts

1. ¬∀x ( M(x) → K(x) )   (Not all monkeys kicked Lewis).  
2. Happy(L) (irrelevant to the options).  
3. [ ∀x ( M(x) → R(x) ) ] ∨ D(L) (Every monkey will run or Lewis is a donkey).  
4. ¬∀x ( H(x) → R(x) )   (Not all humans will run).  
5. ¬S(L , Ha)   (Lewis doesn’t see Hazel).  
6. There is at least one donkey, one human, one monkey.

What the options say

1. ( ∃x ( M(x) ∧ K(x) ) ) ∨ ¬∀x ( M(x) → S(x , Ha) )
2. ¬S(Ha , Ha) ∧ ∀x ( M(x) → K(x) )
3. ¬∀x ( M(x) → K(x) ) ∧ S(L , Ha)
4. ¬∀x ( M(x) → K(x) ) ∧ ¬S(L , Ha)
5. ¬S(Ha , Ha) ∧ ¬∀x ( M(x) → K(x) )
6. S(Ha , Ha) ∧ ¬∀x ( M(x) → K(x) )

Checking which must be true

• 4 is the conjunction of two facts that are explicitly given (1 and 5).  
  Therefore 4 is true in every model of the premises.

• 1 need not be true.  A consistent model exists in which no monkey kicked Lewis
  and every monkey saw Hazel, making 1 false.

• 2 cannot be true in any model because its second conjunct
  (all monkeys kicked Lewis) contradicts the given “not all monkeys kicked Lewis”.

• 3 is impossible because it asserts S(L , Ha), contradicting the given
  ¬S(L , Ha).

• 5 and 6 each contain an undetermined statement about Hazel seeing herself, so
  they can be either true or false, hence are not guaranteed.

Conclusion

The only statement that must be true is statement 4.

Final answer: 4.

question.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a donkey, or a human, or a monkey (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. No monkeys kicked Hazel. A monkey kicked a donkey that kicked a donkey. Every human is happy and Lewis isn't a donkey.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. Lewis is happy.
2. Hazel kicked a monkey.
3. A monkey kicked Hazel.
4. Every monkey kicked Hazel.
5. Not all monkeys kicked Hazel.
6. Hazel kicked every monkey.

response.raw_response:
The information we are given is:

a) Every creature is ​either​ a donkey, a human or a monkey (no overlaps) and there is at least one of each kind.

b) No monkey kicked Hazel.
   (For every x, if x is a monkey then x did ​not​ kick Hazel.)

c) A monkey kicked a donkey that kicked a donkey.
   (∃m∃d₁∃d₂  [m is a monkey ∧ d₁, d₂ are donkeys ∧ m kicked d₁ ∧ d₁ kicked d₂].)

d) Every human is happy.
   (For every x, if x is a human then x is happy.)

e) Lewis is not a donkey. (So Lewis is either a human or a monkey.)

–––  Checking the six candidate conclusions  –––

1. “Lewis is happy.”  
   Lewis is happy only if Lewis is a human.  
   Premises allow Lewis to be a monkey instead, so Lewis need ​not​ be happy.  
   → Not necessarily true.

2. “Hazel kicked a monkey.”  
   There is no premise forcing Hazel to kick anyone at all.  
   We can build a model in which Hazel never kicks a monkey.  
   → Not necessarily true.

3. “A monkey kicked Hazel.”  
   This directly contradicts premise (b): no monkey kicked Hazel.  
   → Necessarily false, hence not a must-true statement.

4. “Every monkey kicked Hazel.”  
   Also contradicts premise (b) (there is at least one monkey that did ​not​ kick Hazel).  
   → Necessarily false.

5. “Not all monkeys kicked Hazel.”  
   From (b) we know ​no​ monkey kicked Hazel, and we are told there is at least one monkey.  
   Therefore “not all monkeys kicked Hazel” is unavoidably true.  
   → Must be true.

6. “Hazel kicked every monkey.”  
   Nothing in the premises forces Hazel to kick even one monkey; we can build a model in which she kicks none.  
   → Not necessarily true.

–––  Conclusion  –––

Of the six statements, the only one that must be true in every situation satisfying the premises is

Statement 5.  “Not all monkeys kicked Hazel.”

question.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a donkey, or a human, or a monkey (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. A monkey saw a human that saw a monkey. Lewis saw every human. No donkeys chased Alfred just in case every monkey chased Hazel. If not all donkeys kicked Alfred, then Hazel kicked Alfred only if not all donkeys kicked Hazel. If Alfred didn't kick Hazel, then every monkey kicked Lewis only if no monkeys kicked Hazel.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. No humans saw Lewis.
2. Lewis saw a human.
3. A human saw Lewis.
4. Not all humans saw Lewis.
5. Alfred is a donkey.
6. Every human saw Lewis.

response.raw_response:
Let  

• D(x), H(x), M(x) mean ‘x is a donkey / human / monkey’ (the three sorts are exclusive and each sort is non-empty);  
• S(x,y)  ‘x saw y’; C(x,y)  ‘x chased y’; K(x,y)  ‘x kicked y’.  

The information given can be formalised as

1. ∃m∃h (M(m) ∧ H(h) ∧ S(m,h) ∧ ∃m′(M(m′) ∧ S(h,m′)))      (a monkey saw a human that saw a monkey)

2. ∀h (H(h) → S(Lewis , h))                                      (Lewis saw every human)

3. [∀d (D(d) → ¬C(d, Alfred))] ↔ [∀m (M(m) → C(m, Hazel))]     (equivalence about ‘chased’)

4. [¬∀d (D(d) → K(d, Alfred))] → [K(Hazel , Alfred) → ¬∀d (D(d) → K(d, Hazel))]

5. [¬K(Alfred , Hazel)] → [[∀m (M(m) → K(m, Lewis))] → ∀m (M(m) → ¬K(m, Hazel))].

The six candidate conclusions are

1.  ∀h (H(h) → ¬S(h, Lewis))
2.  ∃h (H(h) ∧ S(Lewis , h))
3.  ∃h (H(h) ∧ S(h , Lewis))
4.  ¬∀h (H(h) → S(h , Lewis))
5.  D(Alfred)
6.  ∀h (H(h) → S(h , Lewis)).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Step 1.  What must be true?

Because the population contains at least one human and because Lewis sees every human (statement 2 above), there certainly exists a human whom Lewis sees.  Hence

∃h (H(h) ∧ S(Lewis , h))            (candidate statement 2)

is forced in every model.  So statement 2 must be true.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Step 2.  What need **not** be true?

To see that none of the remaining five statements is forced, it suffices to build a single model in which all premises hold while each of those five statements can be individually falsified.

Take the domain  

 Donkeys:  d₁  
 Humans :  h₁ (= Alfred)  
 Monkeys:  m₁  
 plus Lewis and Hazel as separate individuals of the required sorts (say Lewis = d₁, Hazel = m₁; the exact choices do not matter provided each individual is given exactly one sort).

Define the relations as follows.

• Seeing: S(m₁ , h₁) and S(h₁ , m₁) (satisfies clause 1)  
      S(Lewis , h₁) (satisfies clause 2).  
      Nobody else sees anybody.

• Chasing:  let every monkey chase Hazel and no donkey chase Alfred  
      (this makes both sides of clause 3 true).

• Kicking:  let every donkey kick Alfred (so “not all donkeys kicked Alfred” is false)  
      and let Alfred kick Hazel (so the antecedent of clause 5 is false).  
      No other kicks occur.

All five clauses (1)–(5) are satisfied, yet

1. Some human (h₁) could be arranged to see Lewis or not—so statement 1 can fail.  
3. No human needs to see Lewis—so statement 3 can fail.  
4. It can be arranged that every human sees Lewis—so statement 4 can fail.  
5. Alfred has been declared a human, not a donkey—so statement 5 fails.  
6. If we remove S(h₁ , Lewis) from the model, not every human sees Lewis—so statement 6 can fail.

Since each of the five disputed statements can be made false while all the premises remain true, none of them is logically forced.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Conclusion.

The only statement that must hold in every situation meeting the given conditions is:

2. Lewis saw a human.

question.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a donkey, or a human, or a monkey (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. Not all donkeys ate. No donkeys kicked Hazel and Lewis isn't a donkey. A monkey kicked Hazel unless not all donkeys will run.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. Hazel kicked a donkey.
2. Lewis will run.
3. A donkey kicked Hazel.
4. Every donkey kicked Hazel.
5. Not all donkeys kicked Hazel.
6. Hazel kicked every donkey.

response.raw_response:
Only statement 5 must be true.

Reasoning

1. “Hazel kicked a donkey.”  
   The premises say nothing about whom Hazel kicked.  We can build a model in which Hazel did and a model in which she didn’t, so this statement is not forced.

2. “Lewis will run.”  
   We are told only that Lewis is not a donkey; the rules give no information about whether non-donkeys run.  The statement can be either true or false.

3. “A donkey kicked Hazel.”  
   Premise: “No donkeys kicked Hazel.”  Therefore this statement is necessarily false, not necessarily true.

4. “Every donkey kicked Hazel.”  
   Again contradicted by “No donkeys kicked Hazel.”  It is necessarily false.

5. “Not all donkeys kicked Hazel.”  
   Because there is at least one donkey and no donkey kicked Hazel, it follows that at least one donkey failed to kick Hazel.  Hence this statement is invariably true in every model that satisfies the premises.

6. “Hazel kicked every donkey.”  
   Nothing in the premises constrains Hazel’s actions toward donkeys, so the statement may be true or false.

Thus the only sentence that the given information forces to be true is statement 5.

6. Alfred will run.

response.raw_response:
From the facts we are explicitly told

• Lewis is a donkey and Lewis is asleep.  
 → therefore a donkey (Lewis) is asleep.

None of the other given facts (the conditional about Hazel, the clause about humans not running, the statement that Alfred is asleep, and the species–exhaustion rule) force any further connection between “being a donkey” and “being asleep.”  
In particular, we are not told

– what species Alfred belongs to;  
– whether any other creatures are or are not asleep;  
– whether all donkeys, or only some, are asleep.

Hence we can construct consistent models in which  
• only donkeys are asleep, or  
• no other donkeys are asleep, or  
• every donkey is asleep, etc.,

so none of statements 1, 2, 3, 5, or 6 is forced by the information.

What is forced is simply that at least one donkey is asleep.

Therefore the only statement that must be true is

4. A donkey is asleep.

question.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a donkey, or a human, or a monkey (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. Lewis or Alfred are asleep. Every donkey saw every human. A human saw a human that saw a monkey.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. No donkeys saw a human.
2. Every donkey saw a human.
3. Not all monkeys kicked Lewis and every monkey kicked Lewis.
4. No donkeys saw every human.
5. There is a donkey that every human saw.
6. Not all monkeys kicked Lewis and every monkey kicked Lewis.

response.raw_response:
The given facts are:

F1  Every individual is exactly one of: donkey, human or monkey, and there is at least one of each.  
F2  Lewis or Alfred (possibly both) is asleep.  
F3  Every donkey saw every human.  
F4  A human saw a human who saw a monkey.

A statement “must be true’’ if it is forced by F1–F4 in every possible model that satisfies those facts.

--------------------------------------------------
Check each proposed statement
--------------------------------------------------

1.  No donkeys saw a human.  
    Directly contradicts F3.  Not forced – in fact impossible.

2.  Every donkey saw a human.  
    F3 says every donkey saw every human.  Since there is at least one human (F1), this certainly implies that every donkey saw a human.  
    Forced – must be true.

3.  Not all monkeys kicked Lewis and every monkey kicked Lewis.  
    The two conjuncts are mutually contradictory (there is at least one monkey by F1).  
    No model can make this conjunction true; hence it is not forced by F1–F4.

4.  No donkeys saw every human.  
    Contradicts F3.  Not forced (indeed, always false).

5.  There is a donkey that every human saw.  
    F3 is one-way (donkeys → humans).  Nothing in F1–F4 makes humans look at donkeys.  
    Easy counter-model: one donkey D, one human H, one monkey M with relations  
         D saw H (to satisfy F3),  
         H saw H and H saw M (to satisfy F4),  
         nobody saw D.  
    Statement 5 is false in that model, so it is not forced.

6.  Not all monkeys kicked Lewis and every monkey kicked Lewis.  
    Same contradiction as in 3.  Not forced (indeed impossible).

--------------------------------------------------
Conclusion
--------------------------------------------------

The only statement that is guaranteed by the premises is

2. Every donkey saw a human.

No other listed statement is entailed by the given information.

question.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a donkey, or a human, or a monkey (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. A monkey chased Alfred and a monkey kicked Alfred. Every donkey chased Alfred unless every donkey kicked Lewis. Alfred didn't kick Hazel only if Lewis chased Lewis. Not all donkeys drank if and only if every monkey chased Hazel. No humans are hungry just in case every monkey chased Hazel.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. Alfred and Lewis drank.
2. No humans are hungry unless only donkeys drank.
3. No humans are hungry or only donkeys drank.
4. Not all donkeys drank just in case no humans are hungry.
5. Not all humans are hungry and every donkey drank.
6. Alfred drank.

response.raw_response:
Let  

D(x)  =  “x is a donkey”  
H(x)  =  “x is a human”  
M(x)  =  “x is a monkey”  
C(x,y)=  “x chased y”  
K(x,y)=  “x kicked y”  
R(x)  =  “x drank”  
G(x)  =  “x is hungry”

Names: Alfred, Lewis, Hazel.

The information given translates as

1.  ∀x (D(x) ∨ H(x) ∨ M(x)) and the three predicates are pair-wise exclusive; there is at least one of each species.

2.  ∃x (M(x) ∧ C(x,Alfred))                      (a monkey chased Alfred)

3.  ∃x (M(x) ∧ K(x,Alfred))                     (a monkey kicked Alfred)

4.  (¬∀x(D(x) → K(x,Lewis))) → ∀x(D(x) → C(x,Alfred))  
    (“Every donkey chased Alfred unless every donkey kicked Lewis”.)

5.  (¬K(Alfred,Hazel)) → C(Lewis,Lewis)          (Alfred didn’t kick Hazel only if Lewis chased himself)

6.  (∃x(D(x) ∧ ¬R(x))) ↔ ∀x(M(x) → C(x,Hazel))  
    (“Not all donkeys drank”  iff  “every monkey chased Hazel”.)

7.  (∀x(H(x) → ¬G(x))) ↔ ∀x(M(x) → C(x,Hazel))  
    (“No humans are hungry”  iff  “every monkey chased Hazel”.)

From (6) and (7) we obtain immediately

   (∃x(D(x) ∧ ¬R(x)))  ↔  (∀x(H(x) → ¬G(x)))      … (∗)

In words:  
Not all donkeys drank  just in case  no humans are hungry.

Now examine each candidate statement.

1.  “Alfred and Lewis drank.”  
    There are models of the premises in which neither of them drinks (e.g. let Alfred be a donkey that didn’t drink and make the biconditionals (6) and (7) true by having every monkey chase Hazel).  Not forced.

2.  “No humans are hungry unless only donkeys drank.”  
    “Unless” means “if not … then …”.  
    From (∗) we know that no‐human-hungry is equivalent to “not all donkeys drank”, not to “only donkeys drank”, so this implication can fail.  Not forced.

3.  “No humans are hungry or only donkeys drank.”  
    A disjunction is forced only if both disjuncts are forced.  Using a model where some human is hungry and some non-donkey drinks (thus falsifying both disjuncts) shows it is not forced.

4.  “Not all donkeys drank just in case no humans are hungry.”  
    This is exactly statement (∗), which is a logical consequence of the premises.  Therefore it must be true.

5.  “Not all humans are hungry and every donkey drank.”  
    A model with all humans hungry or with a donkey that didn’t drink falsifies this.  Not forced.

6.  “Alfred drank.”  
    Same counter-model as for (1) shows it needn’t be true.

Hence the only statement that must be true in every situation satisfying the given information is

Statement 4.

response.raw_response:

   Nothing in the premises links “not seeing” to being a donkey, so Alfred could equally well be a human or a monkey.  Not forced.

   Same reason as (2); certainly not compelled.

   This flatly contradicts premise 4 (“No donkeys kicked Hazel”), so it cannot be true in any admissible model, let alone be forced.  (It is necessarily false, not necessarily true.)

   Premise 4 says that no donkey kicked Hazel.  If no donkey kicked Hazel, then, a fortiori, it is not the case that all donkeys kicked Hazel.  Hence statement 6 is true in every model that satisfies the premises.

Conclusion  
The only statement that must be true is:

6. Not all donkeys kicked Hazel.

response.raw_response:
Because of the clause  

“Every donkey chased a monkey that chased it”

we have, for every object x:

 if x is a donkey, then there exists some y such that  
  • y is a monkey,  
  • x chased y, and  
  • y chased x.

2. A donkey chased a monkey.  (∃d ∃m [Donkey(d) ∧ Monkey(m) ∧ Chased(d,m)])

Hence, the only statement that must be true is:

Nothing in the premisses implies that no donkey saw Hazel (some may have done so) and nothing allows Hazel not to have seen herself.

Therefore, among the proposed conclusions the only conjunction that is forced to hold in every model is

1. Not all donkeys saw Hazel and Hazel saw Hazel.

All the other options contain a component (“Hazel doesn’t see Hazel” or “no donkeys saw Hazel”) that can be false, so they are not compelled by the premisses.

Final answer: Statement 1, and only statement 1, must be true.

question.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a donkey, or a human, or a monkey (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. Hazel or Lewis are happy. Hazel is hungry. No donkeys will attack just in case Hazel isn't hungry.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. Only donkeys will attack.
2. Not all donkeys will attack.
3. No donkeys will attack.
4. Lewis will attack.
5. A donkey will attack.
6. Every donkey will attack.

response.raw_response:
Let  

D(x)  =  “x is a donkey”  
H(x)  =  “x is a human”  
M(x)  =  “x is a monkey”  
A(x)  =  “x will attack”  
Hun(x) = “x is hungry”  
Happy(x) = “x is happy”  

Premises  

1. ∀x [ D(x) ∨ H(x) ∨ M(x) ] and the three kinds all exist.  
2. Happy(Hazel) ∨ Happy(Lewis).  
3. Hun(Hazel).  
4. “No donkeys will attack just in case Hazel isn’t hungry.”

The phrase “P just in case Q” is standardly read as a biconditional (P ↔ Q).  
So premise 4 is  

¬∃x [D(x) ∧ A(x)]  ↔  ¬Hun(Hazel).          (†)

Step-by-step deduction  

a. From premise 3 we have Hun(Hazel).  
   Hence ¬Hun(Hazel) is false.

b. Because the biconditional (†) is stipulated to be true, both sides must share
   the same truth–value.  
   Since ¬Hun(Hazel) is false, the left-hand side ¬∃x[D(x) ∧ A(x)] must also be false.

c. ¬∃x[D(x) ∧ A(x)] is false precisely when  
   ∃x[D(x) ∧ A(x)] is true; i.e.  

   There exists at least one donkey that will attack.

Consequences for the listed statements  

1. “Only donkeys will attack.” – Need not be true; other species may or may not attack.  
2. “Not all donkeys will attack.” – Need not be true; it is compatible with all
   donkeys attacking.  
3. “No donkeys will attack.” – False by the argument above.  
4. “Lewis will attack.” – Undetermined; nothing forces Lewis to attack.  
5. “A donkey will attack.” – Must be true (proved in (c)).  
6. “Every donkey will attack.” – Need not be true; some donkey’s not attacking is not ruled out.

Therefore the only statement that must be true in every situation satisfying the premises is

Statement 5: “A donkey will attack.”

question.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a donkey, or a human, or a monkey (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. Alfred or Hazel will run. Only donkeys will run. Every monkey saw Hazel. If Alfred will run, then every monkey is asleep only if every human saw Hazel.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. Every donkey will run.
2. A donkey will run.
3. Not all donkeys will run.
4. No donkeys will run.
5. Alfred will run.
6. Alfred and Hazel will run.

response.raw_response:
Facts supplied

F1  Alfred or Hazel will run.  (At least one of them is a runner.)
F2  Only donkeys will run.      (If anything runs, it is a donkey.)
F3  Every monkey saw Hazel. 
F4  If Alfred will run, then (all monkeys are asleep) → (all humans saw Hazel).

Needed: which of the six listed statements is true in every situation (i.e., entailed by F1-F4)?

Step-by-step reasoning

1.  From F1 we know that there exists at least one runner (Alfred, Hazel, or both).

2.  From F2 we know that every runner is a donkey (run(x) → donkey(x)).

3.  Combining 1 and 2 we obtain: there exists at least one donkey that runs.

This single conclusion is all that can be forced by the facts.  Let us now test each candidate statement.

Statement 1   “Every donkey will run.”  
Counter-example: let Hazel be the only runner and be a donkey; let there be another donkey that does not run.  F1-F4 are satisfied, but the statement is false.  Hence not entailed.

Statement 2   “A donkey will run.”  
As shown in steps 1–3, this follows directly from the facts.  Entailed.

Statement 3   “Not all donkeys will run.”  
Counter-example: make every donkey (and only donkeys) run.  F1-F4 are satisfied, so the statement is not forced.  Not entailed.

Statement 4   “No donkeys will run.”  
Contradicted by steps 1–3, so it cannot hold in every model.  Not entailed.

Statement 5   “Alfred will run.”  
Counter-example: let Hazel (a donkey) run while Alfred does not.  F1-F4 are satisfied.  Not entailed.

Statement 6   “Alfred and Hazel will run.”  
Same counter-example as for Statement 5 shows it need not hold.  Not entailed.

Final answer

The only statement that must be true is

2.  A donkey will run.

question.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a donkey, or a human, or a monkey (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. Lewis is a donkey. Every human is happy but it is not the case that Lewis didn't chase Hazel. Hazel isn't a human just in case not all donkeys chased Alfred.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. A donkey chased Hazel.
2. Hazel chased every donkey.
3. Not all donkeys chased Hazel.
4. Every donkey chased Hazel.
5. Hazel chased a donkey.
6. No donkeys chased Hazel.

response.raw_response:
Premises

1. Every creature is **exclusively** either a donkey, a human or a monkey, and there is at least one of each kind.  
2. Lewis is a donkey.  
3. “It is not the case that Lewis didn’t chase Hazel.”  
   (¬¬Chased(Lewis,Hazel) ⇒ Chased(Lewis,Hazel)).  
4. “Hazel isn’t a human just in case not all donkeys chased Alfred.”  
   (¬Human(Hazel) ↔ ¬∀x (Donkey(x) → Chased(x, Alfred))).

Logical consequences of the premises

• From (3) we obtain Chased(Lewis,Hazel).  
• From (2) Lewis is a donkey, so a donkey chased Hazel.

That is the only fact about the “chasing Hazel” relation that is forced by the premises.  
Nothing in the information supplied fixes how any other creature (including Hazel herself) behaves toward Hazel, so every other possibility is still open.

Examining the six candidate statements

1. A donkey chased Hazel.  
   Guaranteed true (Lewis is such a donkey).

2. Hazel chased every donkey.  
   Could be true (if we stipulate it) or false (if we don’t). Not forced.

3. Not all donkeys chased Hazel.  
   Could be false (e.g. if Lewis is the only donkey) or true (if some other donkey does not chase Hazel). Not forced.

4. Every donkey chased Hazel.  
   Same reasoning as (3): could be true or false. Not forced.

5. Hazel chased a donkey.  
   Also undetermined; we can make it true or false at will. Not forced.

6. No donkeys chased Hazel.  
   Impossible, because we already know Lewis (a donkey) chased Hazel. Necessarily false.

Conclusion

The only statement that must be true in every situation satisfying the premises is

1. A donkey chased Hazel.

Statements 2-5 are not determined by the premises, and statement 6 is necessarily false.

question.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a donkey, or a human, or a monkey (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. A human kicked a donkey that kicked a human. A human chased Lewis only if not all humans chased Lewis. If Lewis won't run, then not all donkeys kicked Hazel only if no monkeys kicked Hazel.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. A human chased Lewis.
2. Lewis chased a human.
3. Lewis chased every human.
4. Not all humans chased Lewis.
5. Every human chased Lewis.
6. No humans chased Lewis.

response.raw_response:
The only statement that must be true is  

4. Not all humans chased Lewis.

Explanation (sketch):

• From the condition  
  “A human chased Lewis only if not all humans chased Lewis”  
  we have   
     (∃ h ∈ Humans : h chased Lewis)  →  ¬(∀ h ∈ Humans : h chased Lewis).

  Therefore it is impossible for every human to have chased Lewis, for that would make the left-hand side true and the right-hand side false.  
  Hence “not all humans chased Lewis” is inevitable in every admissible situation.

• The premises place no further compulsory restrictions on who does or does not chase Lewis (or on what Lewis himself chases).  
  Consistent models exist in which  
     – some but not all humans chased Lewis, or  
     – no human chased Lewis at all.  

  Thus none of the other listed statements (1, 2, 3, 5, 6) is forced to hold in every model.

Consequently, statement 4 is the single conclusion that must always be true.

question.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a donkey, or a human, or a monkey (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. Every monkey saw Hazel. If a human chased Lewis, then every monkey saw Lewis only if Lewis is hungry. A monkey is hungry but it is not the case that Hazel saw Hazel.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. Hazel is hungry.
2. Lewis isn't hungry.
3. Hazel isn't a monkey.
4. Hazel is a monkey.
5. Lewis and Hazel are hungry.
6. Lewis is hungry.

response.raw_response:
The only thing that the information forces on Hazel, Lewis and the others is that  

• Hazel cannot be a monkey.

Reasoning:

1. “Every monkey saw Hazel.”  
   Therefore, for any individual x, if x is a monkey then x saw Hazel.

2. At least one monkey exists.  

3. “Hazel did not see Hazel.”  

   If Hazel herself were a monkey, clause 1 would apply to her (because the
   universal statement includes every monkey), forcing her to have seen herself –
   contradicting clause 3.  Hence Hazel cannot be a monkey.

Nothing in the premises fixes whether Hazel (or Lewis) is hungry, nor does it
require Lewis to be (or not be) hungry, because the conditional concerning
Lewis’ hunger is only triggered if some human chased Lewis and if, in addition,
every monkey saw Lewis – conditions that are not guaranteed.

Therefore, among the given options, the one that must be true is

3. Hazel isn't a monkey.

All the other statements could be true in some models and false in others, so
they are not logically necessary.

question.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a donkey, or a human, or a monkey (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. No humans are asleep. Not all monkeys are asleep. No humans are asleep only if not all humans chased Lewis.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. Only humans are asleep.
2. Lewis is asleep.
3. Every human is asleep.
4. Not all humans are asleep.
5. Not all humans are asleep if and only if a human is asleep.
6. A human is asleep.

response.raw_response:
Facts we are given  

F1. Every creature is either a donkey, a human or a monkey and there is at least one of each.  
F2. No humans are asleep.  (∀x)(Human(x) → ¬Asleep(x))  
F3. Not all monkeys are asleep.  (∃x)(Monkey(x) ∧ ¬Asleep(x))  
F4. “No humans are asleep only if not all humans chased Lewis.”  
    “P only if Q” means P → Q, so with P = “No humans are asleep” and P actually true (F2),
    we can conclude ¬(all humans chased Lewis) but that tells us nothing about sleep.

Now test each candidate statement.

1. Only humans are asleep  (Asleep(x) → Human(x))  
   Could be false if some donkey or monkey happens to be asleep.  Not forced.

2. Lewis is asleep  
   We have no information at all about Lewis’s state.  Not forced.

3. Every human is asleep  
   Directly contradicts F2.  Cannot be true.

4. Not all humans are asleep  
   Because F2 says no human is asleep and we know at least one human exists,
   the statement “all humans are asleep” is false, so “not all humans are asleep” is necessarily true.

5. Not all humans are asleep iff a human is asleep  
   Left side is true (see 4).  Right side is false (F2).  Hence the biconditional is false.  
   So it is not a necessary truth.

6. A human is asleep  
   Contradicts F2.  Cannot be true.

Therefore the only statement that must be true in the described situation is:

Statement 4.

question.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a donkey, or a human, or a monkey (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. Alfred and Hazel are asleep. A human saw a monkey that saw a donkey. Not all donkeys will run but it is not the case that Lewis is a donkey.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. Not all donkeys will run but it is not the case that Alfred isn't asleep.
2. Alfred and Hazel will run.
3. Alfred will run.
4. Alfred and Lewis will run.
5. Alfred won't run.
6. Not all donkeys will run and Alfred isn't asleep.

response.raw_response:
We ask which options are true in every model that satisfies the given information.

Facts supplied:

F1  Domain-exhaustion  
    ∀x (Donkey(x) ∨ Human(x) ∨ Monkey(x)), these three predicates are pair-wise exclusive, and ∃x Donkey(x), ∃x Human(x), ∃x Monkey(x).

F2  Asleep(Alfred) and Asleep(Hazel).

F3  ∃h,m,d [Human(h) ∧ Monkey(m) ∧ Donkey(d) ∧ Saw(h,m) ∧ Saw(m,d)].

F4  ¬∀x (Donkey(x) → WillRun(x))   (i.e. “not all donkeys will run” – at least one donkey will not run).

F5  ¬Donkey(Lewis).

From F5 and F1 we can only conclude that Lewis is either a human or a monkey; we are told nothing about whether he (or anyone else) will run.  
Similarly, nothing links the predicate Asleep with WillRun.

Now evaluate each candidate statement:

1. “Not all donkeys will run but it is not the case that Alfred isn’t asleep.”  
   This is simply “¬∀x(Donkey(x)→WillRun(x)) ∧ Asleep(Alfred).”  
   Both conjuncts are exactly F4 and part of F2, hence this statement is true in every model that satisfies the premises.

2. “Alfred and Hazel will run.”  
3. “Alfred will run.”  
4. “Alfred and Lewis will run.”  
5. “Alfred won’t run.”  
   None of 2-5 is forced: the premises place no restriction at all on WillRun for Alfred, Hazel or Lewis, so one can construct models where they do or do not run.

6. “Not all donkeys will run and Alfred isn’t asleep.”  
   The first part is true (F4) but the second contradicts F2, so statement 6 is false in every model.

Therefore the only statement that must be true, given the information, is statement 1.

Final answer: Statement 1 only.

question.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a donkey, or a human, or a monkey (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. A monkey ate. No monkeys saw a monkey. Every human chased every human that chased it. Every human saw every monkey that saw every donkey. No donkeys chased Alfred if and only if a human saw Hazel.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. There is a monkey that every monkey saw.
2. A monkey saw every monkey.
3. A monkey saw a monkey.
4. Every monkey saw every monkey.
5. A monkey saw every monkey that saw it.
6. Every monkey saw a monkey.

response.raw_response:
The only statement that is forced to hold is statement 5.

Reasoning:

• “No monkeys saw a monkey’’ means that for any two monkeys (even the same one) the predicate Saw( … , … ) is always false when both arguments are monkeys.

• Statement 5 says:  “A monkey saw every monkey that saw it.’’
   In logical form  
        ∃m [ Monkey(m) ∧ ∀y ( Monkey(y) ∧ Saw(y , m) → Saw(m , y) ) ].

   Because there is at least one monkey, choose any monkey m.  
   For every monkey y the antecedent “Monkey(y) ∧ Saw(y , m)” is false (no monkey ever sees a monkey), so the implication is true for every y.  
   Thus the bracketed condition is satisfied for that m, and statement 5 is true in every model of the given premises.

• Statements 1, 2, 3, 4 and 6 all require some monkey–monkey seeing to occur, but the premise “No monkeys saw a monkey’’ makes that impossible. Hence each of those statements can be false in a model that satisfies all the given information, so none of them is forced to be true.

Therefore, the statement that must be true is:

5. A monkey saw every monkey that saw it.

question.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a donkey, or a human, or a monkey (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. A human is happy and a monkey is happy. No humans chased Hazel only if not all humans chased Lewis. Not all donkeys chased Alfred and only donkeys ate.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. Alfred ate.
2. A human is happy just in case not all donkeys chased Alfred.
3. Alfred didn't eat.
4. Alfred and Hazel ate.
5. Alfred and Lewis ate.
6. A human is happy just in case no donkeys chased Alfred.

response.raw_response:
We translate the information into predicates.

D(x): x is a donkey  
H(x): x is a human  
M(x): x is a monkey (exclusive partition, each kind exists)

Happy(x): x is happy  
Ch(x,y): x chased y  
Eat(x): x ate  

Premises  

P1  ∀x [ D(x) ⊕ H(x) ⊕ M(x) ]   (exclusive) and each sort is non–empty.  
P2  ∀x [ H(x) → Happy(x) ] and ∀x [ M(x) → Happy(x) ].  
P3  (No humans chased Hazel) → (Not-all humans chased Lewis).  
P4a ¬∀x [ D(x) → Ch(x , Alfred) ]        (not all donkeys chased Alfred).  
P4b ∀x [ Eat(x) → D(x) ]                 (only donkeys ate).

We check each candidate statement; a statement is “must-be-true’’ only if it holds in every model that satisfies P1–P4.

1. Alfred ate.  
Nothing ever makes Alfred eat; we can build a model in which no one eats at all.  So 1 is not forced.

3. Alfred didn’t eat.  
Conversely we can build a model in which Alfred (a donkey) does eat.  So 3 is not forced.

4. Alfred and Hazel ate.  
Take Hazel to be a human (humans never have to eat) and nobody eats; the premises are still satisfied, so 4 is not forced.

5. Alfred and Lewis ate.  
Same idea as (4); not forced.

6. A human is happy just in case no donkeys chased Alfred.  
Humans are always happy (P2).  We can let some donkey chase Alfred, so “no donkeys chased Alfred’’ is false while “a human is happy’’ is true, breaking the biconditional.  Hence 6 is not forced.

2. A human is happy just in case not all donkeys chased Alfred.  
Premises already give both halves:  
– There exists a human and, by P2, every human is happy.  
– P4a states “not all donkeys chased Alfred.’’  
Because BOTH sides of the biconditional are guaranteed true in every model, the biconditional itself is guaranteed true.  Whether we read “a human’’ existentially (“∃x H(x)∧Happy(x)”) or generically (“∀x (H(x)↔…)”), the statement is entailed by the premises.

Therefore the only statement that must be true is

2. A human is happy just in case not all donkeys chased Alfred.

question.question:

Everything is a donkey, or a human, or a monkey (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. A monkey drank. A human saw every monkey. If every human is asleep, then Alfred will run only if every monkey saw Hazel.

6. No humans saw every monkey.

response.raw_response:
Let us translate the information into first-order logic.

Sorts  
D(x) … x is a donkey  
H(x) … x is a human  
M(x) … x is a monkey  
Exactly one of D, H, M holds of every object, and each of the three sorts is non-empty.

Given facts  
(1) ∃m [M(m) ∧ Drank(m)]                           (“A monkey drank.”)  
(2) ∃h [H(h) ∧ ∀m (M(m) → Saw(h,m))]     (“A human saw every monkey.”)  
(3) (∀h (H(h) → Asleep(h))) → (Run(Alfred) → ∀m (M(m) → Saw(m,Hazel)))
     (“If every human is asleep, then Alfred will run only if every monkey saw Hazel.”)

We now test the six candidate conclusions; a conclusion is “must be true” iff it holds in **every** model satisfying (1)–(3).

Candidate 1:  ∃h∃m [H(h) ∧ M(m) ∧ Saw(h,m)]  
Because (2) supplies a human h that sees every monkey and the domain contains at least one monkey, the pair (h,m) witnesses the existential.  
⇒ Always true.

Candidate 2:  ∃h [H(h) ∧ ∀m (M(m) → Saw(m,h))]  
Nothing we are told forces any monkey to have seen a particular human. A model in which no monkey sees any human satisfies the premises, so this statement need **not** hold.  
⇒ Not forced.

Candidate 3:  ¬∃h∃m [H(h) ∧ M(m) ∧ Saw(h,m)]  
This is the negation of candidate 1, which we have just shown is always true; hence 3 is always false.  
⇒ Not forced.

Candidate 4:  ∀h∀m [(H(h) ∧ M(m)) → Saw(h,m)]  
Premises say only that one specific human sees every monkey; other humans might see none.  
⇒ Not forced.

Candidate 5:  ∀h [H(h) → ∃m (M(m) ∧ Saw(h,m))]  
Again, nothing obliges every human to see a monkey; only one such human is guaranteed.  
⇒ Not forced.

Candidate 6:  ¬∃h [H(h) ∧ ∀m (M(m) → Saw(h,m))]  
This directly contradicts premise (2), so it cannot be true in every model.  
⇒ Not forced.

Conclusion  
The only statement that must be true, given the information provided, is:

1. A human saw a monkey.

    reads (in logical form):
        If no human is asleep  →  not all humans chased Lewis.

premises.

Why none of the others is forced

•  It is consistent with the premises that some human did chase Lewis
   and also consistent that none did, so 1 and 5 are not compelled.

•  Because 2 is necessarily true, statement 3 (“Every human chased
   Lewis”) is necessarily false.

•  The premises say nothing about who Lewis chases, so 4 and 6 can be
   either true or false in different admissible scenarios.

Consequently, the only statement that must be true is statement 2.

question.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a donkey, or a human, or a monkey (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. No humans kicked a donkey. Every monkey kicked Lewis and a monkey is happy. If every monkey kicked Lewis, then not all humans kicked Hazel.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. No humans kicked Hazel.

4. Hazel kicked a human.
5. Not all humans kicked Hazel.

response.raw_response:
Let  

D(x): x is a donkey  
H(x): x is a human  
M(x): x is a monkey  
K(x,y): x kicked y  
Lewis and Hazel are particular individuals.

Given facts  

F1  Everything is (exclusively) a donkey or a human or a monkey, and there is at least one of each.  
F2  ¬∃x∃y (H(x) ∧ D(y) ∧ K(x,y))        (No human kicked a donkey.)  
F3  ∀x (M(x) → K(x, Lewis))             (Every monkey kicked Lewis.)  
F4  ∃x (M(x) ∧ Happy(x))                (A monkey is happy – irrelevant to kicking.)  
F5  [∀x (M(x) → K(x, Lewis))] → ¬∀x (H(x) → K(x, Hazel))  
     (If every monkey kicked Lewis, then not all humans kicked Hazel.)

Step-by-step deduction  

1. From F3 we have the antecedent of F5: ∀x (M(x) → K(x, Lewis)).  
2. By modus ponens with F5, conclude ¬∀x (H(x) → K(x, Hazel)).  
3. Since F1 guarantees that at least one human exists, ¬∀x (H(x) → K(x, Hazel)) is equivalent to  
   ∃x (H(x) ∧ ¬K(x, Hazel)), i.e. “Not all humans kicked Hazel.”

So the following must be true:

5. Not all humans kicked Hazel.

Testing the other options shows they are not forced:

• It is consistent with the premises that no human kicked Hazel (making 1 true)  
  and equally consistent that some human did kick Hazel (making 1 false).  

• Options 2, 3, 4, 6 can all be made true or false by choosing the kicking
  relation suitably while respecting F2–F5, so none of them are necessary.

Therefore, the only statement that must be true is

5. Not all humans kicked Hazel.

question.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a donkey, or a human, or a monkey (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. No monkeys saw Alfred. There is a human that every donkey saw. Hazel doesn't see Hazel but it is not the case that Lewis doesn't see Hazel.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. Alfred doesn't see Lewis unless not all monkeys saw Hazel.
2. Alfred doesn't see Hazel just in case no monkeys saw Lewis.
3. Every monkey saw Alfred and Lewis doesn't see Hazel.
4. Every monkey saw Alfred but it is not the case that Lewis doesn't see Hazel.
5. No monkeys saw Alfred just in case Lewis saw Hazel.
6. Alfred doesn't see Lewis or not all monkeys saw Hazel.

response.raw_response:
Must-true statements are the ones that follow from the given facts in every model that satisfies them.  
Let  

D(x)  =  “x is a donkey”  
H(x)  =  “x is a human”  
M(x)  =  “x is a monkey”  
S(x,y)=  “x saw y”  

with constants a (Alfred), h (Hazel) and l (Lewis).

Facts supplied

1. ∀x (M(x) → ¬S(x,a))                (no monkey saw Alfred)  
2. ∃y (H(y) ∧ ∀x(D(x) → S(x,y)))      (some human was seen by every donkey)  
3. ¬S(h,h)                            (Hazel doesn’t see herself)  
4. S(l,h)                            (Lewis sees Hazel)  
5. Everything is exactly one of donkey, human, monkey and there is at least one of each.

Test each candidate statement.

1 and 6  
‘Alfred doesn’t see Lewis unless …’ and ‘Alfred doesn’t see Lewis or …’ are equivalent to  
(∀x(Mx → Sx h)) → ¬S(a,l).  
A counter-model: one monkey m sees Hazel; Alfred sees Lewis.  
All premises are satisfied but the conditional is false, so 1 and 6 are not forced.

2  
(¬S(a,h)) ↔ (∀x(Mx → ¬S(x,l))).  
Make Alfred see Hazel while some monkey does not see Lewis; the biconditional is false although all premises are true.  
Thus 2 is not forced.

3  
‘Every monkey saw Alfred …’ contradicts fact 1, so 3 is impossible, hence not forced.

4  
First conjunct again contradicts fact 1, so 4 is impossible, hence not forced.

5  
‘No monkeys saw Alfred just in case Lewis saw Hazel’ is  
(∀x(Mx → ¬S(x,a))) ↔ S(l,h).  
Left side is exactly fact 1 (always true); right side is fact 4 (always true).  
True ↔ True is necessarily true in every model of the premises.  
Therefore statement 5 must hold.

Conclusion

The only statement that is forced by the information given is

5. No monkeys saw Alfred just in case Lewis saw Hazel.

None of the other five statements must be true.

response.raw_response:

2. It is not the case that Lewis saw Alfred (i.e., Lewis did not see Alfred).

Statement 3 is exactly the conjunction of those two facts, so it must be true.  
Every other listed statement contains at least one claim that contradicts the given information (e.g., that Lewis saw Alfred or that Hazel saw Hazel), so none of them is forced to be true.

question.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a donkey, or a human, or a monkey (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. No humans are hungry if and only if Lewis saw Hazel. If Alfred doesn't see Lewis, then every monkey saw Lewis only if every monkey is hungry. A human saw Lewis but it is not the case that Lewis doesn't see Hazel.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. No humans are hungry.
2. Only humans are hungry and not all humans are hungry.
3. Alfred is hungry.
4. Every human is hungry.
5. Only humans are hungry.
6. A human is hungry.

response.raw_response:
Let  

H(x) … x is a human  
M(x) … x is a monkey  
D(x) … x is a donkey             (exactly one of the three for every creature)  
Hun(x) … x is hungry  
Saw(x , y) … x saw y  

Names:  Lewis = L, Hazel = Z, Alfred = A.

Premises (transcribed literally)

P1.  (∀x(H(x) → ¬Hun(x)))  ↔  Saw(L , Z)              (No humans are hungry  iff  Lewis saw Hazel)

P2.  ¬Saw(A , L)  → ( (∀x(M(x) → Saw(x , L))) → (∀x(M(x) → Hun(x))) )
    (If Alfred doesn’t see Lewis, then: “every monkey saw Lewis → every monkey is hungry”.)

P3.  ∃x( H(x) ∧ Saw(x , L) )          (A human saw Lewis)
     ¬¬Saw(L , Z)                      (“it is not the case that Lewis doesn’t see Hazel”)

     The second half of P3 is simply Saw(L , Z).

--------------------------------------------------------
Step 1.  From P3 we have Saw(L , Z).

Step 2.  Plug this into the biconditional P1:  

        Saw(L , Z) is true  ⇒  (∀x(H(x) → ¬Hun(x))) is true.

        Hence:  No human is hungry.     (*)

Step 3.  Nothing else in P2 or P3 can force any human to be hungry,
         and P2 talks only about Alfred and monkeys, so (*) holds in every
         model that satisfies all the premises.

--------------------------------------------------------
Now test each candidate statement.

1. No humans are hungry.  
   Exactly the sentence we proved in (*) – it must be true.

2. Only humans are hungry and not all humans are hungry.  
   “Only humans are hungry” (∀x(Hun(x) → H(x))) can be falsified
   by letting some monkey be hungry, something allowed by the premises.
   Therefore the whole conjunction need not be true.

3. Alfred is hungry.  
   Alfred’s hunger is never fixed by the premises; he may or may not be hungry.

4. Every human is hungry.  
   Contradicts (*) and is therefore impossible, so it certainly is not forced true.

5. Only humans are hungry.  
   Again can be falsified by making some monkey or donkey hungry, so not forced.

6. A human is hungry.  
   Violates (*), hence cannot be true in any model and is not forced true.

--------------------------------------------------------
Conclusion

The only statement that must be true in every situation satisfying the
premises is statement 1.

Final answer: 1 only.

Everything is a donkey, or a human, or a monkey (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. No donkeys kicked Hazel but it is not the case that Hazel doesn't see Hazel. If Hazel kicked Hazel, then every monkey saw Hazel only if no monkeys kicked Hazel. Not all humans will run and every donkey will run.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. Hazel doesn't see Hazel and a human will run.
2. Hazel will run.
3. Hazel saw Hazel and a human will run.
4. Hazel saw Hazel and no humans will run.
5. Hazel saw Hazel and only humans will run.
6. Hazel saw Hazel and not all humans will run.

response.raw_response:
We translate the information into first-order predicates.

Sorts  
D(x) : x is a donkey  
H(x) : x is a human  
M(x) : x is a monkey               (exactly one of these holds for every object)  
Run(x) : x will run  
Kick(x,y) : x kicked y  
See(x,y) : x saw y  

Given facts  

F1. ∀x ( D(x) → ¬Kick(x,Hazel) )                no donkey kicked Hazel  
F2. See(Hazel,Hazel)                             Hazel saw herself  
F3. Kick(Hazel,Hazel) → ( (∀x(M(x)→See(x,Hazel))) → (∀x(M(x)→¬Kick(x,Hazel))) )  
F4. ¬∀x ( H(x) → Run(x) )                       not all humans will run  
      (equivalently ∃x ( H(x) ∧ ¬Run(x) ) )  
F5. ∀x ( D(x) → Run(x) )                         every donkey will run  
F6. There is at least one donkey, one human and one monkey.

We test each candidate statement:  

1. ¬See(Hazel,Hazel) ∧ ∃x(H(x)∧Run(x))  
   The first conjunct contradicts F2.  Statement 1 is therefore impossible and is
   certainly not forced to be true.

2. Run(Hazel)  
   Let Hazel be a human who does not run; take some other human who also does not
   run, one donkey that runs (by F5) and one monkey.  
   All given facts are satisfied, yet Run(Hazel) is false.  
   So 2 is not forced to be true.

3. See(Hazel,Hazel) ∧ ∃x(H(x)∧Run(x))  
   In the model just described, no human runs, so the second conjunct is false.
   Hence 3 is not forced to be true.

4. See(Hazel,Hazel) ∧ ∀x(H(x)→¬Run(x))  
   Modify the model so that one human does run; all premisses still hold (F4 only
   requires at least one non-running human).  
   Thus 4 can be false, so it is not forced.

5. See(Hazel,Hazel) ∧ ∀x(Run(x)→H(x))             “only humans run”  
   Because F6 gives us at least one donkey and F5 says every donkey runs,
   some runner is a donkey, contradicting “only humans run”.  
   Statement 5 is impossible, let alone necessary.

6. See(Hazel,Hazel) ∧ ¬∀x(H(x)→Run(x))           “Hazel saw Hazel and not all humans run”  
   The first conjunct is exactly F2; the second is exactly F4.  
   Both are guaranteed by the premises, therefore Statement 6 is true in every
   admissible model.

Conclusion  
The only statement that must be true is

6. Hazel saw Hazel and not all humans will run.

question.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a donkey, or a human, or a monkey (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. If a monkey chased Hazel, then every donkey saw Hazel. Every monkey ate and Hazel saw Hazel. Hazel saw Alfred but it is not the case that Lewis isn't happy.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. Every monkey ate but it is not the case that Hazel saw Alfred.
2. Not all monkeys ate just in case Alfred doesn't see Hazel.
3. If a monkey saw Alfred, then Hazel ate.
4. A monkey saw Alfred only if Hazel ate.
5. Every monkey ate and Hazel saw Alfred.
6. Alfred doesn't see Hazel just in case a monkey ate.

response.raw_response:
Let  

D(x) = “x is a donkey”,  
H(x) = “x is a human”,  
M(x) = “x is a monkey”,  
Saw(x , y) = “x saw y”,  
Ate(x) = “x ate”,  
Happy(x) = “x is happy”.

The information given is

(i)  ∀x [D(x) ∨ H(x) ∨ M(x)] , the three kinds are exclusive, and there is at least one of each.  
(ii)  (∃m)[M(m) ∧ Chased(m , Hazel)]  →  ∀d[D(d) → Saw(d , Hazel)].  
(iii) ∀x[M(x) → Ate(x)].                       (every monkey ate)  
(iv) Saw(Hazel , Hazel).  
(v) Saw(Hazel , Alfred).  
(vi) Happy(Lewis).                     (¬¬Happy(Lewis))

From (iii) we know:  Every monkey ate.  
From (v)  we know:   Hazel saw Alfred.

Now examine each candidate statement.

1.  “Every monkey ate but Hazel did not see Alfred.”  
    Second conjunct contradicts (v).  Statement (1) is false, hence not guaranteed.

2.  “Not all monkeys ate  iff  Alfred doesn’t see Hazel.”  
    The left side is false (all monkeys did eat).  The right side can be either true or false
    because nothing fixes whether Alfred sees Hazel.  The biconditional therefore can
    be true in some models and false in others; it is not forced.

3.  “If a monkey saw Alfred, then Hazel ate.”  
    Hazel’s eating is completely unconstrained.  
    A model where some monkey saw Alfred and Hazel did not eat is compatible with the
    premises, so (3) need not hold.

4.  “A monkey saw Alfred only if Hazel ate.”  
    Logically  (∃x[M(x) ∧ Saw(x , Alfred)]) → Ate(Hazel).  
    The same counter-model as for (3) shows it is not forced.

5.  “Every monkey ate and Hazel saw Alfred.”  
    First conjunct is premise (iii); second conjunct is premise (v).  
    Both are given, so the conjunction is true in every model of the premises.  
    Statement (5) must be true.

6.  “Alfred doesn’t see Hazel  iff  a monkey ate.”  
    A monkey certainly ate (there is at least one monkey and all monkeys ate), so the
    right-hand side is always true.  The biconditional therefore collapses to
    ¬Saw(Alfred , Hazel).  Since nothing fixes whether Alfred sees Hazel, the statement
    is not forced.

Therefore the only statement that must be true in every situation satisfying the given information is

Statement 5.

question.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a donkey, or a human, or a monkey (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. Every donkey chased a monkey that chased it. Hazel doesn't see Hazel but it is not the case that Lewis doesn't see Hazel. A human saw Lewis and only donkeys ate.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. A human saw Lewis and Lewis doesn't see Hazel.
2. A human saw Hazel just in case Lewis saw Hazel.
3. Lewis saw Lewis just in case not all humans saw Hazel.
4. Lewis saw Lewis just in case a human saw Hazel.
5. A human saw Lewis and Hazel saw Hazel.
6. A human saw Lewis but it is not the case that Hazel saw Hazel.

response.raw_response:
Let  

D(x) = “x is a donkey”,  
H(x) = “x is a human”,  
M(x) = “x is a monkey”,  
C(x,y) = “x chased y”,  
S(x,y) = “x saw y”,  
E(x)   = “x ate”.

The premisses translate as

1.  Every individual is exactly one of D, H, M and each sort is non-empty.  
2.  ∀x(D(x) → ∃y(M(y) ∧ C(x,y) ∧ C(y,x))).  
3.  ¬S(Hazel , Hazel).  
4.  S(Lewis , Hazel).                (because “it is not the case that Lewis doesn’t see Hazel”)  
5.  ∃x( H(x) ∧ S(x , Lewis)).        (“A human saw Lewis …”)  
6.  ∀x( E(x) → D(x)).               (“only donkeys ate”).

From (3) and (5) we already have

A.  Hazel does not see Hazel.  
B.  Some human saw Lewis.

Now check each candidate statement.

1.  “A human saw Lewis and Lewis doesn’t see Hazel.”  
    The second conjunct contradicts (4).  Hence the whole statement is false in every admissible model and therefore is **not** forced to be true.

2.  “A human saw Hazel iff Lewis saw Hazel.”  
    The right-hand side is always true (by (4)), but the existence of a human who saw Hazel is not guaranteed; models in which no human saw Hazel are allowed.  So the biconditional can be false, hence it is **not** forced to be true.

3.  “Lewis saw Lewis iff not all humans saw Hazel.”  
4.  “Lewis saw Lewis iff a human saw Hazel.”  
    In both cases it is easy to build models (choose the seeing relation at will) where the left and right sides disagree, so neither biconditional is forced to be true.

5.  “A human saw Lewis and Hazel saw Hazel.”  
    The second conjunct contradicts (3), so the whole sentence is always false; it is **not** forced to be true.

6.  “A human saw Lewis and it is not the case that Hazel saw Hazel.”  
    This is exactly the conjunction of the facts we already have in A and B, so it holds in every admissible model; it **must** be true.

Therefore the only statement that is forced by the given information is

Statement 6.

question.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a donkey, or a human, or a monkey (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. Every monkey kicked Lewis. A monkey kicked every donkey that kicked it. A human is asleep unless Lewis kicked Lewis. If no humans are asleep, then Hazel didn't chase Lewis. If not all humans are asleep, then Hazel kicked Alfred only if not all donkeys kicked Hazel.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. Not all monkeys kicked Lewis.
2. No monkeys kicked Lewis.
3. Lewis kicked a monkey.
4. A monkey kicked Lewis.
5. Lewis kicked every monkey.
6. Alfred is asleep.

response.raw_response:
Premises (formalised)

P1  Everyone is exactly one of: donkey, human, monkey; there is at least one of each kind.  
P2  Every monkey kicked Lewis:      ∀m( Monkey(m) → Kick(m, Lewis) ).  
P3  If a donkey kicked a monkey, that monkey kicked the donkey:
     ∀d∀m( Donkey(d) ∧ Monkey(m) ∧ Kick(d,m) → Kick(m,d) ).  
P4  If Lewis did not kick himself, every human is asleep:
     ¬Kick(Lewis, Lewis) → ∀h( Human(h) → Asleep(h) ).  
P5  If no humans are asleep, Hazel did not chase Lewis:
     (¬∃h( Human(h) ∧ Asleep(h) )) → ¬Chase(Hazel, Lewis).  
P6  If some human is awake, then
     (Hazel kicked Alfred) → (¬∀d( Donkey(d) → Kick(d, Hazel) )).

What must be true?

Statements to test

1. Not all monkeys kicked Lewis.  
2. No monkeys kicked Lewis.  
3. Lewis kicked a monkey.  
4. A monkey kicked Lewis.  
5. Lewis kicked every monkey.  
6. Alfred is asleep.

Logical consequences

• From P1 there is at least one monkey.  
• From P2 every monkey kicked Lewis.

Therefore there certainly exists at least one monkey that kicked Lewis.

So statement 4 (“A monkey kicked Lewis”) is true in every model satisfying the premises.

The other statements are not forced.

– Statement 1 contradicts P2, so it cannot be true in any model, let alone in all of them.  
– Statement 2 likewise contradicts P2 and the fact that a monkey exists.  
– For statement 3 we can build a model in which Lewis never kicks any monkey (the premises never mention Lewis kicking monkeys), so it is not necessary.  
– Statement 5 is still weaker: nothing in the premises obliges Lewis to kick even one monkey, let alone every monkey.  
– For statement 6 we can make Alfred a donkey (or a monkey) and stipulate that he is not asleep; all premises are still satisfied. Hence Alfred’s being asleep is not forced.

Conclusion

The only statement that must be true, given the information provided, is:

4. A monkey kicked Lewis.
