Nothing else in the story puts any constraint on whether Alfred drank, whether any monkey is (or isn’t) hungry, etc.

Now examine the candidate conclusions:

1. “Alfred didn’t drink.”  – Not forced; Alfred may or may not have drunk.  
2. “Alfred isn’t hungry and every monkey drank.” – Both halves are explicitly among the given facts, hence this must be true.

question.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a donkey, or a human, or a monkey (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. No donkeys saw Lewis. Lewis saw Alfred and a human will run. Alfred didn't kick Hazel only if Lewis chased Lewis.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. No donkeys saw Lewis but it is not the case that Lewis saw Alfred.
2. Lewis is a donkey or Alfred doesn't see Lewis.
3. Lewis is a donkey unless Alfred doesn't see Lewis.
4. If Alfred doesn't see Alfred, then a donkey saw Lewis.
5. Lewis is a donkey or it is not the case that Alfred doesn't see Lewis.
6. No donkeys saw Lewis and Lewis saw Alfred.

response.raw_response:
Facts we are told to assume  

A. Every creature is (exclusively) a donkey, a human, or a monkey, and there is at least one of each  
B. ∀x (Donkey(x) → ¬Saw(x, Lewis))     (no donkey saw Lewis)  
C. Saw(Lewis, Alfred)             (Lewis saw Alfred)  
D. ∃x (Human(x) ∧ Will-Run(x))        (a human will run)  
E. ¬Kick(Alfred, Hazel) → Chase(Lewis, Lewis)

What must be true is whatever is logically forced by A–E.

Examination of the six candidate statements

1. “No donkeys saw Lewis but it is not the case that Lewis saw Alfred.”  
   The first clause is true (by B) but the second directly contradicts C.  
   Not forced – in fact it is false.

2. “Lewis is a donkey or Alfred doesn’t see Lewis.”  
3. “Lewis is a donkey unless Alfred doesn’t see Lewis.”  
   Both boil down to the disjunction Donkey(Lewis) ∨ ¬Saw(Alfred, Lewis).  
   A model in which Lewis is a human and Alfred does see Lewis satisfies A–E and
   makes this disjunction false, so neither 2 nor 3 is forced.

4. “If Alfred doesn’t see Alfred, then a donkey saw Lewis.”  
   By B the consequent ‘a donkey saw Lewis’ is impossible, so this statement would
   be true only when Alfred does see himself.  Nothing in A–E forces that, so 4 is
   not forced; it can be false in a model where Alfred does not see himself.

5. “Lewis is a donkey or it is not the case that Alfred doesn’t see Lewis.”
   This is Donkey(Lewis) ∨ Saw(Alfred, Lewis).  
   A model with Lewis a human and Alfred not seeing Lewis satisfies A–E and makes
   5 false, so it is not forced.

6. “No donkeys saw Lewis and Lewis saw Alfred.”  
   The first conjunct is exactly B and the second is exactly C, both given facts.
   Hence their conjunction is unavoidably true in every model of A–E.

Conclusion

The only statement that must be true in the described situation is statement 6.

is true in every possible situation that satisfies the given premises.

question.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a donkey, or a human, or a monkey (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. No donkeys saw Alfred. A donkey kicked Lewis. A human saw Lewis but it is not the case that Hazel saw Hazel.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

2. Every donkey saw Alfred.
3. Alfred saw a donkey.
4. A donkey saw Alfred.
5. Not all donkeys saw Alfred.
6. Alfred saw every donkey.

response.raw_response:
Premises

1. Every individual is exactly one of: donkey, human, monkey; each group is non-empty.
2. ∀x (Donkey(x) → ¬Saw(x, Alfred))            (No donkey saw Alfred)
3. ∃x (Donkey(x) ∧ Kicked(x, Lewis))           (Some donkey kicked Lewis)
4. ∃x (Human(x) ∧ Saw(x, Lewis))               (Some human saw Lewis)
5. ¬Saw(Hazel, Hazel)                          (Hazel didn’t see herself)

Names (Alfred, Lewis, Hazel) are ordinary constants; they may or may not denote the same individual.

Required: which statements are true in every model that satisfies the premises?

Analysis of each candidate statement

1. Alfred is a donkey.  
   No premise fixes Alfred’s species.  Alfred can be a human or a monkey in a model, so this is not guaranteed.

2. Every donkey saw Alfred.  
   Premise 2 says exactly the opposite—no donkey saw Alfred—and we know there is at least one donkey.  So this statement is never true, let alone necessarily true.

3. Alfred saw a donkey.  
   The premises place no constraint on what Alfred saw.  We can build a model where Alfred saw nothing, so the statement is not forced.

4. A donkey saw Alfred.  
   Premise 2 forbids any donkey from seeing Alfred, so this statement is always false, not always true.

5. Not all donkeys saw Alfred.  
   Because (a) there is at least one donkey and (b) no donkey saw Alfred (premise 2), it is impossible that all donkeys saw Alfred.  
   Therefore “Not all donkeys saw Alfred” is true in every model.

6. Alfred saw every donkey.  
   Again, the premises do not tell us what Alfred saw; we can make a model where Alfred saw no one.  Hence this statement is not compelled.

Conclusion

The only statement that must be true given the premises is:

5. Not all donkeys saw Alfred.

question.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a donkey, or a human, or a monkey (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. There is not a donkey that every donkey chased. Every donkey chased every donkey that chased every human. Not all monkeys will run but it is not the case that Hazel didn't chase Alfred.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. A donkey chased a human.
2. A donkey chased every human.
3. Every donkey chased a human.
4. Every donkey chased every human.
5. No donkeys chased every human.
6. No donkeys chased a human.

response.raw_response:
Let  

D(x)  = “x is a donkey”  
H(x)  = “x is a human”  
M(x)  = “x is a monkey” (the three kinds are exclusive and all exist)  
C(x,y)= “x chased y”.

The information given translates as

P1.  ¬∃d ( D(d) ∧ ∀k ( D(k) → C(k,d) ) )  
     (There is no donkey that every donkey chased.)

P2.  ∀d₁ ( D(d₁) → ∀d₂ ( ( D(d₂) ∧ ∀h ( H(h) → C(d₂,h) ) ) → C(d₁,d₂) ) )  
     (Every donkey chased every donkey that chased every human.)

P3.  (irrelevant here: some monkeys don’t run)

P4.  C(Hazel, Alfred).

We examine the six candidate conclusions.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
5.  No donkey chased every human
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Assume, toward contradiction, that some donkey d* chased every human:

     D(d*) ∧ ∀h ( H(h) → C(d*,h) ).

Plugging d* into P2: for every donkey k, C(k,d*).  
Hence d* is a donkey chased by every donkey, contradicting P1.  
Therefore such a donkey cannot exist, so

     ¬∃d ( D(d) ∧ ∀h ( H(h) → C(d,h) ) )

is forced by the premises.  Statement 5 must be true.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1, 2, 3, 4, 6 are not forced
--------------------------------------------------------------------
To show that the other statements are not entailed, construct one model
in which the premises hold but those statements can fail.

Objects:  
 d (a donkey)  h (a human)  m/Hazel (a monkey)  Alfred (a human)

Chase relation: only C(Hazel, Alfred) is true; no donkey chases anyone.

Check the premises:  
• P1 holds because the only donkey, d, is not chased by d.  
• P2 holds vacuously: no donkey chases every human, so the “if” part of the implication is always false.  
• P3 is satisfied by letting m be a monkey that doesn’t run.  
• P4 is satisfied by the single chase pair.

Now evaluate the candidate statements in this model:

1. “A donkey chased a human” – false (d chased no-one).  
2. “A donkey chased every human” – false.  
3. “Every donkey chased a human” – false.  
4. “Every donkey chased every human” – false.  
5. “No donkey chased every human” – true (as proved above).  
6. “No donkeys chased a human” – true in this model, but it can be
   false in other models (e.g., if Hazel were a donkey and Alfred a human).

Because we have at least one model in which 1, 2, 3, 4, 6 are false while all premises remain true, none of those statements is logically forced.  
Statement 5, however, is true in every model that satisfies the premises.

Final answer: Only statement 5 must be true.

question.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a donkey, or a human, or a monkey (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. Not all donkeys chased Alfred. A human will run and every human saw Alfred. If every monkey chased Lewis, then not all monkeys chased Lewis.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. Alfred won't run.
2. Alfred and Lewis will run.
3. Lewis will run.
4. A human saw Alfred just in case not all donkeys chased Alfred.
5. Alfred will run.
6. A human saw Alfred if and only if no donkeys chased Alfred.

response.raw_response:
Only statement 4 is forced to be true; the other five need not be true.

Reasoning

Facts we are given:
1. The universe is partitioned into donkeys, humans and monkeys, each non-empty.
2. Not all donkeys chased Alfred → there exists a donkey that did not chase Alfred.
3. Every human runs.
4. Every human saw Alfred.
5. “If every monkey chased Lewis, then not all monkeys chased Lewis.”
   For a material conditional P → ¬P to be true, P must be false; hence  
   ¬ ( every monkey chased Lewis ) → there exists a monkey that did not chase Lewis.

What the six candidate statements say:

1. Alfred won’t run (¬Run(Alfred)).
   – This can be false: let Alfred be a human; all humans run.

2. Alfred and Lewis will run.
   – Can fail if, say, both are donkeys (no rule says donkeys run).

3. Lewis will run.
   – Can fail if Lewis is a donkey or monkey that does not run.

4. A human saw Alfred just in case (iff) not all donkeys chased Alfred.
   – We already know
     a) There is at least one human, and every human saw Alfred ⇒ a human saw Alfred.
     b) Not all donkeys chased Alfred (premise).
     Both sides of the biconditional are therefore always true, so the biconditional itself is
     necessarily true in every model satisfying the premises.

5. Alfred will run.
   – Can fail if Alfred is a donkey or monkey that doesn’t run.

6. A human saw Alfred iff no donkeys chased Alfred.
   – Left side is always true (see 4a); the right side can be false (some donkeys may have
     chased Alfred), so the biconditional need not hold.

Therefore the only statement that must hold in every situation compatible with the premises is statement 4.

response.raw_response:
The facts we are told to accept as true are  

F1. Everything is either a donkey, a human or a monkey, and there is at least one of each.  
F2. Every donkey will run. (∀x)(Donkey(x) → Run(x))  
F3. Hazel kicked a donkey. (∃y)(Donkey(y) ∧ Kicked(Hazel , y))  
F4. No donkeys kicked Hazel. (∀x)(Donkey(x) → ¬Kicked(x , Hazel))  
F5. Alfred is happy.

We must decide which candidate statements are true in **every** interpretation that satisfies F1–F5.

• Statement 2: “A donkey will run.”  
Because F1 guarantees at least one donkey exists and F2 says every donkey runs, there is certainly at least one runner. Therefore statement 2 is true in every model.

• Statements 1, 3, 4, 5, 6:

1. “Alfred will run.”  
   Alfred might be a human or a monkey who does not run; the premises place no constraint on this. So it is not forced to be true.

3. “Not all donkeys will run.”

5. “Alfred and Hazel will run.”  
   Hazel will run if she is a donkey, but she could be a human or monkey; Alfred’s running is likewise unconstrained. Hence the conjunction is not guaranteed.

6. “Only donkeys will run.”

Hence the sole statement that must be true is:

Statement 2.  A donkey will run.

question.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a donkey, or a human, or a monkey (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. Not all donkeys saw Hazel and a monkey kicked Alfred. A monkey kicked Hazel only if every donkey saw Hazel. If Alfred ate, then no humans are hungry only if every monkey kicked Lewis. If not all donkeys saw Hazel, then not all donkeys kicked Alfred only if Hazel kicked Alfred. If Hazel is hungry, then not all donkeys kicked Alfred only if every human saw Hazel.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. Hazel kicked a monkey.
2. A monkey kicked Hazel.
3. Every monkey kicked Hazel.
4. Alfred ate.
5. No monkeys kicked Hazel.
6. Hazel kicked every monkey.

response.raw_response:
Let

D(x): x is a donkey  
H(x): x is a human  
M(x): x is a monkey  
Saw(x,y): x saw y  
Kick(x,y): x kicked y  
Eat(x): x ate  
Hungry(x): x is hungry  

Translate the five given facts.

F1  ¬∀x(D(x) → Saw(x,Hazel))                        (“Not all donkeys saw Hazel”)  
     ∧ ∃x(M(x) ∧ Kick(x,Alfred))                (“Some monkey kicked Alfred”)

F2  [∃x(M(x) ∧ Kick(x,Hazel))] → [∀x(D(x) → Saw(x,Hazel))]  
     (“A monkey kicked Hazel only if every donkey saw Hazel”)

F3  Eat(Alfred) →
        {[¬∃x(H(x) ∧ Hungry(x))] → [∀x(M(x) → Kick(x,Lewis))]}

F4  ¬∀x(D(x) → Saw(x,Hazel)) →
        {[¬∀x(D(x) → Kick(x,Alfred))] → Kick(Hazel,Alfred)}

F5  Hungry(Hazel) →
        {[¬∀x(D(x) → Kick(x,Alfred))] → [∀x(H(x) → Saw(x,Hazel))]}

-------------------------------------------------
Step 1.  From F1 we already have ¬∀x(D(x) → Saw(x,Hazel)).

Step 2.  Use the contrapositive of F2.

        ¬∀x(D(x) → Saw(x,Hazel))  →  ¬∃x(M(x) ∧ Kick(x,Hazel))

        Because the antecedent holds (Step 1), the consequent follows:

        ¬∃x(M(x) ∧ Kick(x,Hazel))

        “No monkey kicked Hazel.”

So the proposition

  P5:  No monkeys kicked Hazel

is forced to be true in every model of the five facts.

-------------------------------------------------
Step 3.  Show no other listed statement is forced.

Construct a model that satisfies F1–F5 but makes P1, P2, P3, P4, P6 false.

Objects: D1, D2 (donkeys); H1=Hazel, H2=Alfred, H3=Lewis (humans); M1 (monkey)

Interpretation
• Saw(D2,Hazel) true; Saw(D1,Hazel) false (so not all donkeys saw Hazel).  
• Kick(M1,Alfred) true (so a monkey kicked Alfred).  
• Kick(x,Hazel) false for every x (so no monkey kicked Hazel).  
• All donkeys (D1,D2) kick Alfred, no one else does.  
• Hazel kicks nobody.  
• Eat(Alfred) false; Hungry(Hazel) false.  

One easily checks that F1–F5 all hold under this interpretation, while

P1 “Hazel kicked a monkey”  false  
P2 “A monkey kicked Hazel”  false  
P3 “Every monkey kicked Hazel” false  
P4 “Alfred ate”                        false  
P6 “Hazel kicked every monkey” false  

Hence none of P1, P2, P3, P4 or P6 is forced by the facts.

-------------------------------------------------
Conclusion

The only statement that must be true is

5. No monkeys kicked Hazel.

4. Every monkey chased a donkey.      (∀x)(Monkey(x) → (∃y)(Donkey(y) ∧ Chased(x,y)))

Evaluation of the listed statements

1. “Every human is asleep just in case not all humans are asleep.”
   • Let A be “all humans are asleep.”  
   • The statement is A ↔ ¬A, which is a contradiction – it is false in every possible interpretation.
   • Therefore it is not forced by the premises.

2. “Every donkey chased Hazel.”
   • Hazel is a human (Premise 2).
   • Premise 3 says every donkey chased every human.
   • Hence every donkey chased Hazel.  This follows unconditionally from the premises, so (2) must be true.

3. “Hazel chased a donkey.”
   • The premises say nothing about what any human (including Hazel) chased.
   • A model in which Hazel never chases anyone satisfies all the premises, so (3) is not forced.

4. “No donkeys chased Hazel.”
   • This is the exact negation of (2), and (2) is forced true; therefore (4) is forced false.

5. “Hazel chased every donkey.”
   • Again, the premises give no information about Hazel chasing anything.
   • A model in which Hazel chases no donkeys satisfies the premises, so (5) is not forced.

6. “Not all donkeys chased Hazel.”
   • Premise 3 entails that all donkeys did chase Hazel, so (6) is forced false.

Conclusion

The only statement that must be true, given the premises, is:

2. Every donkey chased Hazel.

question.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a donkey, or a human, or a monkey (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. Every donkey chased every human. A monkey kicked Hazel or a human kicked Lewis. A monkey will attack unless not all monkeys saw Hazel. Every donkey will attack unless not all monkeys saw Hazel. If no humans chased Hazel, then not all humans chased Lewis.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. No donkeys chased a human.
2. There is a donkey that every human chased.
3. Not all monkeys kicked Lewis and every monkey kicked Lewis.
4. Every donkey chased a human.
5. Not all donkeys will run and every donkey will run.
6. No donkeys chased every human.

response.raw_response:
Let  

D(x) : x is a donkey  
H(x) : x is a human  
M(x) : x is a monkey  

C(x,y) : x chased y  
K(x,y) : x kicked y  
A(x)   : x will attack  

Premises  

P1  Everything is exactly one of D, H or M; there is at least one of each.  
P2  ∀x(D(x) → ∀y(H(y) → C(x,y)))                              (every donkey chased every human)  
P3  (∃x)(M(x)∧K(x,Hazel)) ∨ (∃y)(H(y)∧K(y,Lewis))              (monkey kicked Hazel or human kicked Lewis)  
P4  (∀x(M(x)→Saw(x,Hazel))) → (∃x)(M(x)∧A(x))                 (if all monkeys saw Hazel, a monkey will attack)  
P5  (∀x(M(x)→Saw(x,Hazel))) → (∀x)(D(x)→A(x))                 (if all monkeys saw Hazel, every donkey will attack)  
P6  (¬∃y(H(y)∧C(y,Hazel))) → ¬∀y(H(y)→C(y,Lewis))             (if no human chased Hazel, not all humans chased Lewis)

The candidate statements

1  ∀x(D(x) → ∀y(H(y) → ¬C(x,y)))  
2  ∃x(D(x) ∧ ∀y(H(y) → C(y,x)))  
3  ¬∀x(M(x) → K(x,Lewis)) ∧ ∀x(M(x) → K(x,Lewis))  
4  ∀x(D(x) → ∃y H(y) ∧ C(x,y))  
5  ¬∀x(D(x) → Run(x)) ∧ ∀x(D(x) → Run(x))  
6  ¬∃x(D(x) ∧ ∀y(H(y) → C(x,y)))

What must follow from P1–P6?

•  From P1 there is at least one human.  
•  From P2, for every donkey x and every human y, C(x,y) is true.  
   Therefore, for every donkey x there exists at least one human (the one that exists by P1) whom x chased.  
   That is exactly statement 4.

•  Statement 1 is the negation of P2 and is therefore impossible.  
•  Statement 2 talks about humans chasing a donkey; the premises say nothing about this, so it need not be true.  
•  Statements 3 and 5 each conjoin a sentence with its own negation; they can never both be true together, so they are not forced by the premises.  
•  Statement 6 contradicts P2 together with the existence of at least one donkey and one human, hence it is false.

Thus the only statement that must be true in every model of the given information is statement 4.

Final answer: 4 only.

question.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a donkey, or a human, or a monkey (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. Alfred ate. Lewis saw Lewis if and only if no monkeys saw Lewis. Lewis saw Lewis just in case not all humans saw Hazel.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. A human saw Lewis and Hazel is a monkey.
2. No monkeys saw Lewis if and only if a human saw Hazel.
3. Hazel isn't a human just in case no monkeys saw Lewis.
4. Hazel is a human only if every monkey saw Lewis.
5. If Hazel is a human, then every monkey saw Lewis.
6. No monkeys saw Lewis just in case not all humans saw Hazel.

response.raw_response:
First, translate the information that is stated to be true.

1.  Lewis saw Lewis  ⇔  no monkey saw Lewis.  
2.  Lewis saw Lewis  ⇔  not-all humans saw Hazel.

Combining (1) and (2)

  no monkey saw Lewis  ⇔  not-all humans saw Hazel.  (★)

That is the only unconditional consequence of the two given biconditionals.

Now examine each candidate statement.

1.  A human saw Lewis ∧ Hazel is a monkey  
  Not forced: make Hazel a donkey and let no human see Lewis.

2.  no monkey saw Lewis  ⇔  a human saw Hazel  
  Not forced: in a model where no monkey saw Lewis and no human saw Hazel, the left side is true and the right side is false.

3.  Hazel is not a human  ⇔  no monkey saw Lewis  
  Not forced: let Hazel be a human; then the left side is false while the right side (if no monkey saw Lewis) can be true.

4.  Hazel is a human only if every monkey saw Lewis  
5.  If Hazel is a human, then every monkey saw Lewis  
  (4) and (5) say the same thing.  
  Not forced: let Hazel be a human and let at least one monkey fail to see Lewis.

6.  no monkey saw Lewis  ⇔  not-all humans saw Hazel  
  This is exactly the equivalence (★) derived above, so it must be true in every model that satisfies the premises.

Since statements 1-5 can all be falsified in models that respect the given information, none of them are logically necessary, whereas statement 6 is always true.

Final answer:  Only statement 6 must be true.

question.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a donkey, or a human, or a monkey (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. A human kicked every monkey. Every monkey kicked a monkey. Hazel is a monkey and no humans will run.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. A human will run.
2. Only humans will run and no humans will run.
3. Only humans will run and not all humans will run.
4. Not all humans will run.
5. Only humans will run.
6. Every human will run.

response.raw_response:
Let  

• D(x), H(x), M(x) mean “x is a donkey / human / monkey” (these three predicates partition the whole universe and each class is non–empty).  
• R(x) mean “x will run”.  
• K(x,y) mean “x kicked y”.  
• h₀ be “Hazel”.

Given facts  

1.  ∃h [H(h) ∧ ∀y (M(y) → K(h,y))] (“A human kicked every monkey”).  
2.  ∀x (M(x) → ∃y [M(y) ∧ K(x,y)]) (“Every monkey kicked a monkey”).  
3.  M(h₀) (“Hazel is a monkey”).  
4.  ∀x (H(x) → ¬R(x)) (“No humans will run”).  

Because there is at least one human (non-emptiness) and no human runs, we have

 ∃x (H(x) ∧ ¬R(x)),

i.e. “Not all humans will run” is necessarily true.

Now examine each candidate statement.

1. A human will run.  
 Contradicted by fact 4, so it is necessarily false.

2. Only humans will run and no humans will run.  
 The second conjunct is true, the first is not forced (a monkey or donkey could run), so this whole statement is not forced.

3. Only humans will run and not all humans will run.  
 Again the “only humans will run” part is not forced; statement not necessary.

4. Not all humans will run.  
 Must be true (derived above).

5. Only humans will run.  
 Not forced; a model with a running monkey satisfies all premises.

6. Every human will run.  
 Contradicted by fact 4; necessarily false.

A concrete counter-model for 2, 3, 5:  
Universe {Henry (human), Hazel (monkey), Dolly (donkey)};  
Henry kicks Hazel (satisfying 1); Hazel kicks herself (satisfying 2); Hazel runs, nobody else runs.  
All premises hold, but 2, 3, 5 are false.

Hence the single statement that must be true in every situation satisfying the given information is

Statement 4: “Not all humans will run.”

question.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a donkey, or a human, or a monkey (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. If Alfred didn't kick Hazel, then Alfred is asleep. Hazel isn't a human if and only if Alfred didn't chase Hazel. Alfred didn't chase Hazel if and only if no donkeys chased Alfred.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. Hazel isn't a human just in case not all donkeys chased Alfred.
2. Hazel isn't a human if and only if no donkeys chased Alfred.
3. Not all donkeys chased Alfred if and only if every human chased Hazel.
4. Alfred and Hazel are asleep.
5. Alfred is asleep.
6. No donkeys chased Alfred if and only if every human chased Hazel.

response.raw_response:
Let  

D(x)  =  “x is a donkey”  
H(x)  =  “x is a human”  
C(x,y)=  “x chased y”  
K(x,y)=  “x kicked y”  
S(x)  =  “x is asleep”  

A = Alfred, Z = Hazel.

The given information translates to  

(1)  ¬K(A,Z)  →  S(A)  
(2)  ¬H(Z)     ↔  ¬C(A,Z)  
(3)  ¬C(A,Z)  ↔  (∀x)(D(x) → ¬C(x,A))            “no donkeys chased Alfred”

Chain the two biconditionals (2) and (3):

¬H(Z)  
  ↔ ¬C(A,Z)                        (by 2)  
  ↔ (∀x)(D(x) → ¬C(x,A))    (by 3)

Hence

¬H(Z)  ↔  “no donkeys chased Alfred”.               (★)

Now examine each candidate statement.

1.  ¬H(Z) ↔ “not all donkeys chased Alfred”  
 “Not all” means “∃x( D(x) ∧ ¬C(x,A) )”, which is strictly weaker than “no donkeys chased Alfred”.  
 A model with one donkey that does chase Alfred and another that does not shows (★) true while (1) is false.  
 So (1) is not forced.

2.  ¬H(Z) ↔ “no donkeys chased Alfred”  
 This is exactly statement (★).  
 Therefore (2) must be true.

3.  “Not all donkeys chased Alfred” ↔ “every human chased Hazel”  
 The premises contain no condition relating donkeys chasing Alfred to humans chasing Hazel, so (3) can be false; a counter-model exists.

4.  “Alfred and Hazel are asleep”  
 Only ¬K(A,Z) → S(A) is given; Alfred may have kicked Hazel, and Hazel’s sleep is never mentioned.  
 Hence (4) need not hold.

5.  “Alfred is asleep”  
 Same reason as in (4): Alfred might have kicked Hazel, so S(A) is not guaranteed.

6.  “No donkeys chased Alfred” ↔ “every human chased Hazel”  
 Again there is no premise linking these two conditions; a counter-model makes (6) false.

Conclusion:  

The only statement that must be true in every situation satisfying the premises is statement 2.

6. Lewis and Hazel ate.

response.raw_response:
The information that is guaranteed by the stipulations is

• Every monkey ate.                  (given explicitly)

No other fact about Hazel’s or Lewis’s eating (or not eating) is forced by the premises; they can eat or not eat in different admissible situations.

Hence the only option that must always be true is

All the other listed sentences can be false in at least one model that satisfies the premises, so they are not logically forced.

question.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a donkey, or a human, or a monkey (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. A human kicked every monkey that kicked it. Only humans are hungry and no donkeys saw Lewis. If every donkey saw Hazel, then not all humans kicked Hazel. If every monkey saw Lewis, then Lewis is hungry only if every monkey kicked Lewis. Alfred isn't hungry but it is not the case that Lewis isn't hungry.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. Lewis isn't a human.
2. Alfred is hungry.
3. Lewis is a human.
4. Alfred and Lewis are hungry.
5. Lewis and Alfred are hungry.
6. Alfred and Hazel are hungry.

response.raw_response:
The information given yields the following facts:

A.  Only humans are hungry  
  hungry(x)  ⇒  human(x)

B.  Alfred isn’t hungry  
  ¬hungry(Alfred)

C.  It is not the case that Lewis isn’t hungry  
  ¬¬hungry(Lewis)  ⇒  hungry(Lewis)

From C and A:

hungry(Lewis)        (from C)  
hungry(Lewis) ⇒ human(Lewis)   (from A)  
——————————————————————————————  
human(Lewis)

So Lewis must be a human.

No other statement forced by the premises makes Alfred hungry, therefore any option that asserts Alfred’s hunger is false.  
Likewise, the proposition that Lewis is **not** a human is contradicted.

Hence, of the six statements, the only one that must be true is

3. Lewis is a human.

question.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a donkey, or a human, or a monkey (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. No donkeys saw Lewis. A donkey saw a donkey that saw a monkey. Alfred doesn't see Alfred only if a monkey chased Hazel. If Lewis didn't chase Lewis, then not all donkeys kicked Hazel only if every human saw Hazel. If not all donkeys kicked Alfred, then Alfred will run only if every monkey saw Hazel.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. Every donkey saw Lewis.
2. No donkeys saw Lewis and Lewis isn't a donkey.
3. Lewis saw every donkey.
4. Not all donkeys saw Lewis.
5. A donkey saw Lewis.
6. Lewis saw a donkey.

response.raw_response:
From the facts we are given we know for certain that

• There is at least one donkey.  
• No donkey saw Lewis.                (premise)

Because at least one donkey exists and every donkey failed to see Lewis, it follows immediately that it is **not** the case that all donkeys saw Lewis.  Hence

Not-all-donkeys-saw-Lewis  is necessarily true.

That is exactly statement 4.

None of the other statements is forced:

1. “Every donkey saw Lewis’’ is the direct opposite of the premise “No donkey saw Lewis’’ and is therefore impossible, not necessary.

2. “No donkeys saw Lewis’’ (true) **and** “Lewis isn’t a donkey’’ (undetermined).  
   Lewis could be a donkey who simply does not see himself, so the whole conjunction need not be true.

3. “Lewis saw every donkey’’ is unconstrained; the information given never links what Lewis sees to what the donkeys do.

5. “A donkey saw Lewis’’ is ruled out by the premise “No donkey saw Lewis’’ and therefore cannot be guaranteed true.

6. “Lewis saw a donkey’’ is likewise unconstrained and may be false in a permissible model.

Thus, the only statement that must hold in every situation satisfying the premises is

4. Not all donkeys saw Lewis.

question.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a donkey, or a human, or a monkey (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. Not all monkeys saw Lewis. Every donkey saw every donkey that saw every donkey. Hazel didn't chase Alfred and Lewis doesn't see Hazel. Hazel saw Hazel and a human will run. Every human drank just in case no monkeys saw Lewis.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. Hazel didn't chase Alfred but it is not the case that Hazel doesn't see Hazel.
2. Hazel saw Hazel and Alfred didn't chase Alfred.
3. Alfred didn't chase Alfred but it is not the case that Hazel doesn't see Hazel.
4. Alfred didn't chase Alfred and Hazel doesn't see Hazel.
5. Hazel didn't chase Alfred and Hazel doesn't see Hazel.
6. Hazel saw Hazel but it is not the case that Alfred didn't chase Alfred.

response.raw_response:
From the information we are given we can prove only the following concrete facts.

a. Hazel did ​not​ chase Alfred.  
b. Hazel at some time ​saw​ Hazel.  
c. Lewis does ​not​ at present ​see​ Hazel.  
d. There is at least one donkey, one human and one monkey, and not every
   monkey saw Lewis.  
e. The two extra rules about what donkeys see, about running and about
   drinking put no restrictions at all on whether  
   – Hazel now sees Hazel, or  
   – Alfred ever chased Alfred.

Nothing in the premises connects the past–tense relation “saw’’ with the
present–tense relation “see”, and nothing links any of the premises to
whether Alfred ever chased himself.  Consequently

• “Hazel doesn’t see Hazel’’ can be either true or false, and  
• “Alfred didn’t chase Alfred’’ can be either true or false, in a model that
  satisfies every one of the given conditions.

Because the six answer-choices each contain at least one of those two
undetermined statements, every one of them can be made true in some
models and false in others.  Hence none of the six choices is forced by the
premises.

Final answer:  None of the statements (1-6) must be true.

question.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a donkey, or a human, or a monkey (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. Every human saw every human. A donkey saw every monkey that saw it. A donkey saw a human that saw a human. Every human saw every donkey that saw every human. A human saw Alfred just in case not all humans kicked Lewis.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. No humans saw every human.
2. No humans saw a human.
3. Alfred is a donkey.
4. There is not a human that every human saw.
5. A human saw every human.
6. No humans saw a human that saw it.

response.raw_response:
The first premise says

Every human saw every human.

That means: for all individuals h and h′,  
if h is a human and h′ is a human then h saw h′.  
So each human sees every human (including himself / herself).  
From this single fact we can already settle all six candidate conclusions.

1. “No humans saw every human.”  
 False – each human DOES see every human.

2. “No humans saw a human.”  
 False – every human sees (many) humans.

3. “Alfred is a donkey.”  
 Not forced – Alfred could be a human, a donkey, or a monkey; nothing given fixes his species.

4. “There is not a human that every human saw.”  
 False – pick any human h′; by the premise every human sees h′.

5. “A human saw every human.”  
 True – indeed every human sees every human, so certainly some human does.

6. “No humans saw a human that saw it.”  
 False – if h and h′ are humans then h saw h′ and h′ saw h, giving plenty of counter-examples.

Because statements 1, 2, 3, 4 and 6 can be false in perfectly legal interpretations of the premises, while statement 5 is true in every such interpretation, the only sentence that must be true is:

Statement 5.

question.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a donkey, or a human, or a monkey (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. Alfred saw every donkey. Not all donkeys saw Hazel but it is not the case that Lewis saw Alfred. No donkeys saw Hazel but it is not the case that Lewis doesn't see Hazel.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. Not all donkeys saw Alfred.
2. Every donkey saw Alfred.
3. No donkeys saw Alfred.
4. A donkey saw Alfred.
5. Alfred saw a donkey.
6. Alfred is a donkey.

response.raw_response:
Premises that have to be satisfied

P1   Alfred saw every donkey.                        (∀x)(Donkey(x) → Saw(Alfred , x))

P2   Not all donkeys saw Hazel.                      ¬(∀x)(Donkey(x) → Saw(x , Hazel))

P3   Lewis did not see Alfred.                       ¬Saw(Lewis , Alfred)

P4   No donkeys saw Hazel.                           (∀x)(Donkey(x) → ¬Saw(x , Hazel))

P5   Lewis sees Hazel.                               Saw(Lewis , Hazel)

Plus

• Every creature is either a donkey, a human or a monkey – exclusively.
• There is at least one donkey, one human and one monkey.

What must follow from those premises?

(1)–(4) and (6) are not forced

To see this, build two different models that obey every premise but give different
truth-values to the candidate statements.

Model A  
– Species:  two donkeys d1 , d2 ; Alfred a human; Hazel a human; Lewis a monkey.  
– Seeing:   Alfred sees d1 and d2 (so P1 holds); nobody else sees any donkey.  
             No donkey sees Hazel (P4), so P2 also holds;  
             Lewis sees Hazel (P5) and does not see Alfred (P3).

Results in Model A  
1  true   (not all donkeys saw Alfred)  
2  false  (not every donkey saw Alfred)  
3  false  (some donkey could see Alfred)  
4  false  (no donkey actually saw Alfred here)  
5  true   (Alfred saw a donkey)  
6  false  (Alfred is a human)

Model B  
– Same creatures.  
– Seeing:  Alfred still sees d1 and d2; in addition d1 and d2 both see Alfred.  
Everything else as in Model A.

Results in Model B  
1  false  (now every donkey sees Alfred)  
2  true   (every donkey sees Alfred)  
3  false  
4  true   (a donkey saw Alfred)  
5  true  
6  false

All premises are satisfied in both models, yet the truth-values of (1)–(4) vary and (6)
remains false.  Therefore none of (1), (2), (3), (4) or (6) is forced by the premises.

Why (5) is unavoidable

There is at least one donkey, call it d.  
Premise P1 says Alfred sees every donkey, hence Alfred sees d.  
So the existential statement

(∃x)(Donkey(x) ∧ Saw(Alfred , x))

is true in every model that satisfies the premises.  
That is exactly statement 5: “Alfred saw a donkey”.

Conclusion

The only statement that must be true is

5. Alfred saw a donkey.

• Everything is either a donkey, a human or a monkey (no overlaps).

No other given information forces Alfred, Hazel, or anyone else to be (or not be) hungry; models can be built in which Alfred is hungry and models in which he is not.  
Hence the only statement that must be true in every situation satisfying the premises is:

6. Lewis isn't hungry.

question.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a donkey, or a human, or a monkey (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. Not all humans are happy. There is not a human that every monkey saw. A donkey saw a donkey that saw a monkey. No donkeys saw Hazel and Lewis is happy. Hazel doesn't see Alfred or a monkey is hungry.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. A donkey saw Hazel.
2. Every donkey saw Hazel.
3. Not all donkeys saw Hazel and Hazel saw Hazel.
4. Not all donkeys saw Hazel.
5. Hazel saw every donkey.
6. Hazel saw a donkey.

response.raw_response:
Premise (4) says

 “No donkeys saw Hazel.”  
 Formal: ∀x ( Donkey(x) → ¬Saw(x, Hazel) ).

The introductory information says

 “There is at least one donkey.”

Because at least one donkey exists and every donkey fails to see Hazel, it is impossible that *all* donkeys saw Hazel.  
That is exactly statement (4):

 ¬∀x ( Donkey(x) → Saw(x, Hazel) ) “Not all donkeys saw Hazel.”

Hence statement (4) is true in every model that satisfies the premises.

• Statement (1) is impossible (premise 4 forbids any donkey seeing Hazel).  
• Statement (2) is impossible for the same reason.  
• Statement (3) requires “Hazel saw Hazel,” which is not forced by the premises, so it need not hold.  
• Statement (5) and (6) talk about Hazel seeing donkeys; the premises say nothing that guarantees this, so they need not hold.

Therefore the only statement that must be true is:

4. Not all donkeys saw Hazel.

question.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a donkey, or a human, or a monkey (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. If no humans are asleep, then Hazel didn't chase Lewis. Not all donkeys will run but it is not the case that Hazel didn't chase Hazel. Not all humans will run and only donkeys are asleep.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. Not all humans will run and every donkey will run.
2. Not all donkeys will run and no humans will run.
3. Every donkey will run and no humans will run.
4. Lewis will run.
5. Only humans will run and not all donkeys will run.
6. Not all donkeys will run and not all humans will run.

response.raw_response:
Premises (re-stated symbolically)

1. Every creature is exactly one of: donkey (D), human (H) or monkey (M); there is at least one of each kind.

2. If no humans are asleep, then Hazel did not chase Lewis:
      (∀x (H(x) → ¬Asleep(x)))  →  ¬Chase(Hazel, Lewis)

3. Not all donkeys will run: ∃x (D(x) ∧ ¬Run(x))

   Hazel did chase Hazel: Chase(Hazel, Hazel)

4. Not all humans will run: ∃x (H(x) ∧ ¬Run(x))

   Only donkeys are asleep: ∀x (Asleep(x) → D(x))

Immediate consequences of the premises

A. “Only donkeys are asleep” together with the exclusivity of the three species gives  
      H(x) → ¬Asleep(x) for every x, i.e. no human is asleep.

B. Premise 2 therefore has its antecedent true (“no humans are asleep”), so its consequent must be true:  
      ¬Chase(Hazel, Lewis).

C. From Premise 3: ∃x (D(x) ∧ ¬Run(x))     (some donkey will not run)

D. From Premise 4: ∃x (H(x) ∧ ¬Run(x))     (some human will not run)

What must be true?

Now examine each candidate statement against what is forced by A–D.

1. “Not all humans will run and every donkey will run.”  
   The first half is forced (D), but the second half contradicts (C).  Not necessarily true.

2. “Not all donkeys will run and no humans will run.”  
   The first half is forced (C).  The second half (∀x (H(x) → ¬Run(x))) is ​not​ forced—nothing prevents some other humans from running.  Hence not necessarily true.

3. “Every donkey will run and no humans will run.”  
   The first half contradicts (C).  Not true.

4. “Lewis will run.”  
   The premises say nothing about Lewis’s running.  Not forced.

5. “Only humans will run and not all donkeys will run.”  
   The second half holds (C), but the first half (∀x (Run(x) → H(x))) is not entailed.  Not forced.

6. “Not all donkeys will run and not all humans will run.”  
   Both parts are exactly what (C) and (D) state; they are compelled by the premises.

Therefore the only statement that must be true is

Statement 6: Not all donkeys will run and not all humans will run.

question.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a donkey, or a human, or a monkey (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. Every monkey chased every monkey that chased it. A human is happy and a monkey is happy. Hazel saw Hazel and a monkey kicked Alfred.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. Every monkey chased a monkey.
2. No monkeys chased every monkey.
3. Every monkey chased every monkey.
4. A monkey chased every monkey.
5. A monkey chased a monkey.
6. A monkey chased every monkey that chased it.

response.raw_response:
We have to decide which of the six listed conclusions follow from the information given.

Facts provided (formalised):

F1  Partition: every individual is exactly one of Donkey, Human or Monkey; each class is non–empty.

F2  ∀x[Monkey(x) → ∀y((Monkey(y) ∧ Chased(y,x)) → Chased(x,y))]  
    (Every monkey chases back every monkey that chases it.)

F3  Saw(Hazel , Hazel).

F4  ∃m[Monkey(m) ∧ Kicked(m , Alfred)].   (So at least one monkey exists.)

No other constraints on Chased are stated.

Now examine each candidate statement.

1. ∀x[Monkey(x) → ∃y(Monkey(y) ∧ Chased(x,y))]  
   Could be false: take a model with one monkey M and let the Chased relation be empty.  
   All facts F1–F4 hold, but 1 is false.  
   ⇒ not necessarily true.

2. ¬∃x[Monkey(x) ∧ ∀y(Monkey(y) → Chased(x,y))]  
   Could be false: have two monkeys who both chase both (or a single monkey that chases itself).  
   Satisfies F1–F4 yet violates 2.  
   ⇒ not necessarily true.

3. ∀x∀y[(Monkey(x) ∧ Monkey(y)) → Chased(x,y)]  
   Same counter-model as for 1 (empty Chased) shows it needn’t hold.  
   ⇒ not necessarily true.

4. ∃x[Monkey(x) ∧ ∀y(Monkey(y) → Chased(x,y))]  
   The model with empty Chased falsifies this.  
   ⇒ not necessarily true.

5. ∃x∃y[Monkey(x) ∧ Monkey(y) ∧ Chased(x,y)]  
   Again falsified by the empty-Chased model.  
   ⇒ not necessarily true.

6. ∃x[Monkey(x) ∧ ∀y((Monkey(y) ∧ Chased(y,x)) → Chased(x,y))]  
   By F2 every monkey satisfies the property “chases every monkey that chases it”, and by F4 at least one monkey exists.  
   Therefore such a monkey must exist in every model.  
   ⇒ statement 6 is always true.

Conclusion:  
The only statement that must be true is 6.

question.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a donkey, or a human, or a monkey (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. A human saw Alfred. A donkey kicked every donkey. Hazel saw Hazel and no humans will attack.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. Not all humans will attack.
2. Only humans will attack.
3. Alfred will attack.
4. Every human will attack.
5. A human will attack.
6. Alfred and Hazel will attack.

response.raw_response:
We have three exclusive kinds of creatures – donkeys, humans and monkeys – with at least one of each.

Facts given  
• A human saw Alfred.  → at least one human exists.  
• A donkey kicked every donkey.  
• Hazel saw herself.  
• No humans will attack.  → every human is a non-attacker.

Now check every candidate statement.

1. Not all humans will attack.  
   There is at least one human and all humans are non-attackers, so it is certainly **not** the case that every human attacks.  
   Statement 1 is therefore true in every admissible situation.

2. Only humans will attack.  
   The facts say nothing about whether donkeys or monkeys might attack.  We can imagine a model in which a donkey (or a monkey) attacks something; this would satisfy all the premises but make Statement 2 false.  
   Hence it is not forced.

3. Alfred will attack.  
   No information is given about Alfred attacking, so this need not be true.

4. Every human will attack.  
   Directly contradicted by “no humans will attack,” so it cannot be true.

5. A human will attack.  
   Also contradicted by “no humans will attack,” so it cannot be true.

6. Alfred and Hazel will attack.  
   Nothing guarantees either of them attacks, so it is not forced.

Therefore the only statement that must be true is:

Statement 1 – “Not all humans will attack.”

question.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a donkey, or a human, or a monkey (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. Every monkey kicked every monkey. No humans ate just in case not all donkeys drank. Not all donkeys drank if and only if not all humans kicked Lewis.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. Lewis drank.
2. No humans ate if and only if not all humans kicked Lewis.
3. Lewis ate.
4. Lewis didn't eat.
5. Lewis is a donkey.
6. Lewis didn't drink.

response.raw_response:
Let  

P  =  “No humans ate’’  
Q =  “Not all donkeys drank’’  
R  =  “Not all humans kicked Lewis’’ .

The information given is

(1)  P ↔ Q  (“No humans ate just in case not all donkeys drank’’)  
(2)  Q ↔ R  (“Not all donkeys drank iff not all humans kicked Lewis’’).

From the transitivity of logical equivalence we immediately obtain

  P ↔ R.                                       (∗)

That is exactly the content of statement 2:

2. No humans ate if and only if not all humans kicked Lewis.

Hence statement 2 is true in every situation that satisfies the premises.

To see that none of the other statements is forced, build two concrete models of the premises that differ on each of them.

Model A  
• Lewis is a human who does not eat and does not drink.  
• One donkey exists that does not drink; one monkey exists.  
• Every monkey kicks every monkey; the (only) human does not kick Lewis.  
Then P, Q, R are all true; the premises hold, but  
 1 (Lewis drank) is false,  
 3 (Lewis ate) is false,  
 4 (Lewis didn’t eat) is true,  
 5 (Lewis is a donkey) is false,  
 6 (Lewis didn’t drink) is true.

Model B  
• Lewis is a human who eats and drinks.  
• Every donkey (there is at least one) drinks; one monkey exists.  
• Every human (only Lewis) kicks Lewis.  
Then P, Q, R are all false; the premises hold, but  
 1 is true,  
 3 is true,  
 4 is false,  
 5 is false,  
 6 is false.

Since a statement must have the same truth-value in all models to be logically forced, statements 1, 3, 4, 5 and 6 are not entailed (they can be true in one model and false in another).

Therefore, the only statement that must be true is:

Statement 2.

question.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a donkey, or a human, or a monkey (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. Every human kicked Lewis. A monkey saw a human. Alfred is asleep only if Lewis chased Lewis.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. Alfred is asleep.
2. Lewis kicked a human.
3. A human kicked Lewis.
4. No humans kicked Lewis.
5. Lewis kicked every human.
6. Not all humans kicked Lewis.

response.raw_response:
The basic facts we have to respect are  

F1. Every being is (exclusively) a donkey, a human or a monkey, and there is at least one of each.  
F2. For every human h :  h kicked Lewis.  
F3. There exists a monkey m and a human h such that m saw h.  
F4. If Alfred is asleep then Lewis chased Lewis.  (asleep(Alfred) → chased(Lewis, Lewis))

Now test each candidate sentence against every structure that satisfies F1–F4.

1. Alfred is asleep.  
   F4 neither forces Alfred to be asleep nor to be awake (it only says what follows if he is).  
   Possible model: Alfred is awake; all facts are still satisfied.  
   ⇒ Not forced to be true.

2. Lewis kicked a human.  
   F2 tells us about humans kicking Lewis, not about Lewis kicking anyone.  
   Possible model: nobody at all is kicked by Lewis.  
   ⇒ Not forced to be true.

3. A human kicked Lewis.  
   F2 states: for every human h, h kicked Lewis.  
   Because F1 guarantees there is at least one human, there must exist at least one h with Human(h) ∧ kicked(h, Lewis).  
   ⇒ This sentence is true in every model; it is forced.

4. No humans kicked Lewis.  
   Contradicts F2 directly (since there is at least one human).  
   ⇒ Forced to be false, therefore not “must be true”.

5. Lewis kicked every human.  
   Again, F2 gives no information about Lewis as kicker.  
   Possible model: Lewis kicks no one.  
   ⇒ Not forced to be true.

6. Not all humans kicked Lewis.  
   Negation of F2, which says all humans did kick Lewis.  
   ⇒ Forced to be false, therefore not “must be true”.

Conclusion

The only statement that must be true, given the premises, is:

3. A human kicked Lewis.

response.raw_response:
The only thing we are told about the relation between Lewis, Hazel and the verb “see” is

Lewis is a donkey  and  Lewis does not see Hazel.

Because Lewis is certainly a donkey that does ​not​ see Hazel, it is impossible that every donkey sees Hazel.

5. Not all donkeys saw Hazel.

must be true in every model that satisfies the given information.

None of the other proposed statements is forced:

•  Whether any donkey sees Hazel is unconstrained except for Lewis, so 2, 3 and 4 can all be made true or false at will by adding or omitting such a “seeing” relation for some other donkey.

question.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a donkey, or a human, or a monkey (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. Every human chased every human. A monkey chased a human that chased a donkey. If Lewis chased Lewis, then Alfred isn't a human.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. There is not a human that every human chased.
2. No humans chased a human.
3. No humans chased a human that chased it.
4. Alfred is a donkey.
5. No humans chased every human.
6. A human chased every human.

response.raw_response:
Premises  

P1 Every creature is (exclusively) a donkey, a human or a monkey, and there is at least one of each.  
P2 Every human chased every human.  
P3 A monkey chased a human that chased a donkey.  
P4 If Lewis chased Lewis, then Alfred isn’t a human.

We ask which of the six listed statements follows from (P1–P4) in every possible situation that satisfies them.

––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––  
Look at each candidate statement.

1 “There is not a human that every human chased.”  
Because of P2, for any human h and every human k, k chased h.  
So every human is chased by every human.  
Therefore a human that is chased by every human certainly does exist.  
Statement 1 is therefore false in every model → NOT forced.

2 “No humans chased a human.”  
P2 explicitly says the opposite (humans do chase humans).  
So statement 2 is impossible → NOT forced.

3 “No humans chased a human that chased it.”  
With at least one human, P2 makes every pair of humans chase each other (and a lone
human even chases itself), giving a counter-example to statement 3.  
Hence statement 3 is false in every model → NOT forced.

4 “Alfred is a donkey.”  
P4 only tells us that Alfred is not a human if Lewis chases himself; it does not rule out
Alfred’s being human whenever the antecedent is false.  
We can build two models, one in which Lewis does chase himself (so Alfred is not human and could be a donkey) and one in which Lewis does not (so Alfred may be a human).  
Thus Alfred need not be a donkey → statement 4 is NOT forced.

5 “No humans chased every human.”  
P2 guarantees that each human chases every human, so at least one such human certainly exists.  
Statement 5 is therefore false in every model → NOT forced.

6 “A human chased every human.”  
Again by P2, every human chases every human, so in every model at least one human does.  
Statement 6 holds in every possible situation → THIS ONE **IS** forced.

––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––  
Final answer  

The only statement that must be true is:

6. A human chased every human.

Statements 1, 2, 3, 4 and 5 are not guaranteed by the premises.

question.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a donkey, or a human, or a monkey (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. Every human chased every human that chased every monkey. Hazel didn't chase Alfred and every human saw Alfred. No donkeys saw Hazel and every human saw Alfred.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. Alfred didn't chase Alfred and no donkeys saw Hazel.
2. Every human is asleep just in case not all humans are asleep.
3. Not all donkeys saw Hazel but it is not the case that Alfred didn't chase Alfred.
4. Hazel didn't chase Alfred and no donkeys saw Hazel.
5. Not all donkeys saw Hazel but it is not the case that Hazel didn't chase Alfred.
6. Not all donkeys saw Hazel and Alfred didn't chase Alfred.

response.raw_response:
We are told that the following facts hold.

F1  The domain consists **exclusively** of donkeys, humans and monkeys and there is at least one of each.  
F2  Every human chased every human that chased every monkey.  
F3  Hazel didn’t chase Alfred.  
F4  Every human saw Alfred.  
F5  No donkeys saw Hazel.

A statement is “must-be-true’’ iff it is a logical consequence of F1–F5.

Examine each option.

1.  Alfred didn’t chase Alfred ∧ no donkeys saw Hazel  
 The second conjunct is F5 (true), but F1–F5 place no restriction at all on whether Alfred chases himself.  A model in which Alfred does chase himself therefore falsifies the first conjunct, so option 1 is not forced.

2.  “Every human is asleep iff not all humans are asleep’’  
 The vocabulary asleep never occurs in F1–F5.  We can build one model in which all humans are asleep and another in which none are, so the biconditional is not forced.

3.  ¬(all donkeys saw Hazel) ∧ Alfred did chase Alfred  
 Because there is at least one donkey and F5 says no donkey saw Hazel, the first conjunct is true.  The second conjunct is not entailed (see 1), so the whole conjunction is not forced.

4.  Hazel didn’t chase Alfred ∧ no donkeys saw Hazel  
 Both conjuncts are literally F3 and F5.  Hence option 4 is a logical consequence of the facts and therefore must be true.

5.  ¬(all donkeys saw Hazel) ∧ ¬(Hazel didn’t chase Alfred)  
 The first conjunct is true for the same reason as in 3, but the second conjunct contradicts F3, so the whole statement is impossible, let alone necessary.

6.  ¬(all donkeys saw Hazel) ∧ Alfred didn’t chase Alfred  
 Again the first conjunct is true, the second is not entailed, so the conjunction is not forced.

Conclusion:  
The only statement that must be true in every situation satisfying the given information is option 4.

Final answer: 4

question.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a donkey, or a human, or a monkey (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. Lewis saw Hazel. Not all donkeys kicked Hazel. A monkey chased Alfred and every monkey saw Alfred.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. A monkey chased Alfred but it is not the case that Lewis doesn't see Hazel.
2. A monkey chased Alfred and Lewis doesn't see Hazel.
3. If Alfred doesn't see Lewis, then every monkey chased Hazel only if every monkey saw Lewis.
4. Every monkey chased Alfred but it is not the case that Lewis doesn't see Hazel.
5. Alfred didn't chase Hazel just in case no monkeys saw Lewis.
6. Every monkey chased Alfred and Lewis doesn't see Hazel.

response.raw_response:
The given information tells us, and only tells us, that  

• Lewis saw Hazel.  
• There exists at least one monkey that chased Alfred.  
• Every monkey saw Alfred.  
• There is (at least) one donkey that did **not** kick Hazel.  
• Everything is exclusively a donkey, a human or a monkey and there is at least one of each.

From these facts we can be certain of the truth of the following two simple sentences:

(a) Lewis sees Hazel.  
(b) A monkey chased Alfred.

Now examine each candidate statement.

1. “A monkey chased Alfred **but** it is not the case that Lewis doesn’t see Hazel.”  
   – This is exactly the conjunction (b) ∧ (a).  
   – Both conjuncts are entailed by the facts, so statement 1 must be true.

2. “A monkey chased Alfred **and** Lewis doesn’t see Hazel.”  
   – Its second conjunct contradicts fact (a).  It can be false, so it is not forced.

3. “If Alfred doesn’t see Lewis, then every monkey chased Hazel only if every monkey saw Lewis.”  
   – One can construct a model in which Alfred doesn’t see Lewis, every monkey chases Hazel, yet some monkey fails to see Lewis; in that model the statement is false.  
   – Hence it is not forced.

4. “Every monkey chased Alfred **but** it is not the case that Lewis doesn’t see Hazel.”  
   – We are never told that **every** monkey chased Alfred; only that **some** did.  
   – Not forced.

5. “Alfred didn’t chase Hazel **just in case** no monkeys saw Lewis.” (biconditional)  
   – The premises give no link between Alfred-chasing-Hazel and monkeys-seeing-Lewis, so the biconditional can be made true or false at will.  
   – Not forced.

6. “Every monkey chased Alfred **and** Lewis doesn’t see Hazel.”  
   – Second conjunct contradicts fact (a); not forced.

Therefore, the only statement that is guaranteed (must be true) is statement 1.

Final answer: 1 only.

question.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a donkey, or a human, or a monkey (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. Lewis is a donkey. Lewis kicked Hazel. Every monkey kicked Alfred.

response.raw_response:
We are told

• Every individual is either a donkey, a human, or a monkey, and the three kinds are mutually exclusive.  
• There is at least one member of each kind.  
• Lewis is a donkey.  
• Lewis kicked Hazel.  
• Every monkey kicked Alfred.

Now test each candidate statement to see whether it is logically forced by the information.

1. A donkey kicked Hazel.  
   True: Lewis is a donkey and Lewis kicked Hazel; therefore a donkey (Lewis) kicked Hazel. This must hold in every model of the facts.

2. Hazel kicked every donkey.  
   We are told nothing about anyone kicking Hazel, only that Lewis kicked Hazel and monkeys kicked Alfred. Hazel’s actions are completely unspecified, so this need not be true.

3. Hazel kicked a donkey.  
   Again, Hazel’s kicking behaviour is unspecified; the statement is not forced.

4. Not all donkeys kicked Hazel.  
   The facts allow two kinds of models:
   – Lewis is the only donkey, in which case every donkey (namely Lewis) kicked Hazel.  
   – There are additional donkeys, some of whom might not have kicked Hazel.  
   Because both are possible, the truth of “not all donkeys kicked Hazel” is not guaranteed.

5. No donkeys kicked Hazel.  
   False, because Lewis (a donkey) did kick Hazel.

6. Every donkey kicked Hazel.  
   As with (4), this could be true (if Lewis is the only donkey) or false (if there is another donkey that did not kick Hazel). It is therefore not forced.

Therefore the only statement that must be true is:

Statement 1.

question.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a donkey, or a human, or a monkey (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. Hazel is a donkey. No donkeys kicked a monkey. A human chased Hazel only if every donkey saw Hazel.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. A donkey kicked a monkey.
2. A donkey kicked every monkey.
3. Every donkey kicked every monkey.
4. There is not a monkey that every donkey kicked.
5. Every human is asleep just in case not all humans are asleep.
6. Every donkey kicked a monkey.

response.raw_response:
Premises

1. Everything is exactly one of: donkey, human, monkey; and there is at least one of each.
2. Hazel is a donkey.
3. No donkey kicked a monkey: ∀x∀y [Donkey(x) ∧ Monkey(y) → ¬Kicked(x,y)].
4. If a human chased Hazel, then every donkey saw Hazel. (Irrelevant to the “kicked” claims.)

Evaluation of the six candidate conclusions

1. “A donkey kicked a monkey.”  
   Premise 3 says this never happens, so (1) is certainly not forced to be true (it is forced to be false).

2. “A donkey kicked every monkey.”  
   Requires at least one donkey to have kicked at least one monkey, contradicting Premise 3. Hence (2) cannot be true.

3. “Every donkey kicked every monkey.”  
   Also contradicts Premise 3; cannot be true.

4. “There is not a monkey that every donkey kicked.”  
   To have such a monkey m, each donkey would have to kick m, contradicting Premise 3. Therefore the existence of such a monkey is impossible, so statement (4) is necessarily true.

5. “Every human is asleep just in case not all humans are asleep.”  
   Let A = “every human is asleep”; B = “not all humans are asleep.”  
   With at least one human in the domain, A and B are logical negations. The biconditional A ↔ B can never be true (exactly one of A, B holds), so (5) is necessarily false.

6. “Every donkey kicked a monkey.”  
   Again contradicts Premise 3, so (6) is necessarily false.

Conclusion

Of the six statements, the only one that must be true in every model satisfying the given information is:

4. There is not a monkey that every donkey kicked.

All other statements are not forced to be true (indeed, they are forced to be false).

question.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a donkey, or a human, or a monkey (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. Lewis saw Alfred and a monkey kicked Alfred. A human kicked Lewis or every human saw Hazel. Hazel saw Alfred and not all donkeys kicked Alfred.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. Hazel doesn't see Hazel and a monkey kicked Alfred.
2. If a monkey kicked Hazel, then a monkey saw Alfred.
3. If Alfred didn't kick Hazel, then every monkey saw Hazel only if no monkeys kicked Hazel.
4. A monkey kicked Hazel only if a monkey saw Alfred.
5. Hazel saw Alfred and a monkey kicked Alfred.
6. Hazel saw Hazel and a monkey kicked Alfred.

response.raw_response:
The information we are told is:

1. Lewis saw Alfred.  
2. A (at least one) monkey kicked Alfred.  
3. Either a human kicked Lewis, or every human saw Hazel.  
4. Hazel saw Alfred.  
5. Not every donkey kicked Alfred (so there is at least one donkey that did not kick Alfred).  
6. Everything is exclusively a donkey, a human or a monkey, and there is at least one of each.

From this list we can read off two facts that are unconditionally true in every situation that satisfies the description:

• Hazel saw Alfred.  
• A monkey kicked Alfred.

No other definite facts about “seeing” or “kicking” follow logically:  
– We are told nothing about whether Hazel sees herself.  
– We are told nothing that forces any monkey (or anyone else) to see Alfred, apart from Lewis and Hazel.  
– We are told nothing that prevents a monkey from kicking (or not kicking) Hazel, etc.

Hence we examine the six candidate statements:

1. Hazel doesn’t see Hazel and a monkey kicked Alfred.  
   The second part is always true, but we do not know whether Hazel sees herself.  This need not be true.

2. If a monkey kicked Hazel, then a monkey saw Alfred.  
   It is possible that some monkey kicks Hazel while no monkey sees Alfred, so this conditional need not be true.

3. If Alfred didn’t kick Hazel, then (every monkey saw Hazel only if no monkey kicked Hazel).  
   A model in which Alfred didn’t kick Hazel, every monkey saw Hazel, and some monkey also kicked Hazel makes this false.  So it is not forced to be true.

4. A monkey kicked Hazel only if a monkey saw Alfred.  
   As in (2), we can have a monkey kick Hazel while no monkey sees Alfred.  Not necessarily true.

5. Hazel saw Alfred and a monkey kicked Alfred.  
   Both halves are exactly the two unconditional facts we already have, so this statement is true in every model of the premises.

6. Hazel saw Hazel and a monkey kicked Alfred.  
   Again, we do not know whether Hazel sees herself, so this is not guaranteed.

Therefore the only statement that must be true, given the information supplied, is statement 5.

Final answer: 5 only.

question.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a donkey, or a human, or a monkey (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. Hazel saw Lewis. Every monkey ate but it is not the case that Hazel saw Alfred. Hazel saw Hazel and every monkey chased Alfred.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. Alfred didn't eat.
2. Alfred ate.
3. Alfred and Lewis ate.
4. Alfred and Hazel ate.
5. Every monkey ate and Hazel saw Hazel.
6. Every monkey ate but it is not the case that Hazel saw Hazel.

response.raw_response:
Let  

D(x) = “x is a donkey”, H(x) = “x is a human”, M(x) = “x is a monkey”  
Saw(x , y) = “x saw y”, Ate(x) = “x ate”, Chased(x , y) = “x chased y”

Premises  

1. ∀x ( D(x) ∨ H(x) ∨ M(x) ) and the three predicates are pair-wise exclusive; there is at least one object of each kind.  
2. Saw(Hazel , Lewis).  
3. ∀x ( M(x) → Ate(x) ).                    (Every monkey ate.)  
4. ¬ Saw(Hazel , Alfred).                  (Hazel didn’t see Alfred.)  
5. Saw(Hazel , Hazel).                     (Hazel saw herself.)  
6. ∀x ( M(x) → Chased(x , Alfred) ).       (Every monkey chased Alfred.)

What must follow?

• Alfred’s eating status  
   – If Alfred is a monkey, premise 3 forces Ate(Alfred).  
   – If Alfred is a human or a donkey, premise 3 says nothing about him, so Ate(Alfred) may be either true or false.  
   Hence neither “Alfred ate” nor “Alfred didn’t eat” is forced.

• Lewis’s and Hazel’s eating status  
   Lewis could be a monkey (then he ate) or not (then we are free to stipulate Ate(Lewis) true or false).  
   Hazel could be a monkey (ate) or not (may or may not eat).  
   Therefore any statement asserting that Lewis or Hazel definitely ate is not entailed.

Now check each candidate statement.

1. Alfred didn’t eat. Not forced (fails in models where Alfred is a monkey).  
2. Alfred ate. Not forced (fails in models where Alfred is a human or donkey that doesn’t eat).  
3. Alfred and Lewis ate. Not forced (fails whenever 2 fails or Lewis doesn’t eat).  
4. Alfred and Hazel ate. Not forced (fails whenever 2 fails or Hazel doesn’t eat).  
5. Every monkey ate and Hazel saw Hazel. Both conjuncts are exactly the contents of premises 3 and 5, so this is true in every model.  
6. Every monkey ate but Hazel did not see Hazel. Second conjunct contradicts premise 5, so the whole statement is false in every model.

Therefore the only statement that must be true given the premises is

Statement 5: “Every monkey ate and Hazel saw Hazel.”

response.raw_response:

P1  ∃x(Human(x) ∧ Happy(x))                   (a human is happy)

together with the background facts that

Therefore the only statement that must be true in every situation satisfying the given information is

Statement 6: “A human is happy and a monkey chased Alfred.”

P3. “A human will run.” (irrelevant to seeing)  
P4. “Every human saw Alfred.” (Alfred’s species is unspecified)  
P5. “Lewis will attack.” (irrelevant to seeing)

Consequence of P2 and the fact that at least one human exists (P1):  
For every human h and every monkey m, if m saw h then h saw m.  
Because at least one human exists, there is certainly a human for whom that conditional holds – indeed it holds for every human. Hence

 A human saw every monkey that saw it.                      (Statement 2)

None of the other proposed statements is guaranteed:

1. “No humans saw every monkey.”  
   – Could be false if there is only one monkey and every human saw it.

3. “Every human saw every monkey.”  
   – Not forced; if some monkey never saw a given human, that human need not have seen that monkey.

4. “A human saw a monkey.”  
5. “Every human saw a monkey.”  
6. “A human saw every monkey.”  
   – All can fail in a model where no monkey ever sees a human and Alfred is not a monkey (so humans might see only Alfred, a donkey, for example).

Therefore the single statement that must always be true is:

Statement 2.

question.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a donkey, or a human, or a monkey (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. Lewis doesn't see Alfred. Every monkey chased a donkey that chased it. A human kicked a human that kicked a monkey. Every donkey saw Hazel only if not all monkeys chased Lewis.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. No donkeys chased every monkey.
2. No donkeys chased a monkey.
3. Every donkey chased every monkey.
4. Every donkey chased a monkey.
5. A donkey chased every monkey.
6. A donkey chased a monkey.

response.raw_response:
The premises guarantee the existence of (at least) one concrete “donkey-→monkey” chasing pair, and nothing else in the story forces any further pattern of chasing.  

Reasoning:

1.  “There is at least one monkey.”                        (Premise)  

2.  “Every monkey chased a donkey that chased it.”  
    For every monkey m there is some donkey d with  
        donkey(d) ∧ chased(m,d) ∧ chased(d,m).            (Premise)

3.  Combining 1 and 2:  
    Pick one of the (existing) monkeys m.  
    By 2 there is a donkey d such that chased(d,m).  
    Therefore ∃d∃m [donkey(d) ∧ monkey(m) ∧ chased(d,m)].  

    So:  A donkey chased a monkey.                                    (Statement 6)

Nothing else in the description constrains which or how many donkeys do the chasing, so we can build models of the premises that

• have some donkey that chases every monkey (violating 1),  
• have other donkeys that chase no monkeys at all (violating 4),  
• have different donkeys for different monkeys so that no single donkey chases them all (violating 5),  
• etc.  

Thus none of the other candidate statements is forced, while statement 6 is inevitable.

Final answer: 6 only.

question.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a donkey, or a human, or a monkey (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. A human saw a monkey that saw a monkey. Hazel doesn't see Hazel and no donkeys saw Hazel. No donkeys kicked Alfred unless every donkey will attack. Hazel isn't a human only if every donkey saw Hazel. If not all donkeys kicked Alfred, then Alfred saw Alfred only if no monkeys kicked Hazel.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. Every donkey saw a human.
2. No donkeys saw a human.
3. No donkeys saw every human.
4. A donkey saw a human.
5. Every donkey saw every human.
6. A donkey saw every human.

response.raw_response:
Let  

• Donkey(x), Human(x), Monkey(x) – the three exclusive kinds (each non-empty).  
• Saw(x,y) – “x saw y”.  
• Hazel and Alfred – particular individuals.

Given sentences (formalised):

1. ∃h (Human(h) ∧ ∃m₁ (Monkey(m₁) ∧ Saw(h,m₁) ∧ ∃m₂ (Monkey(m₂) ∧ Saw(m₁,m₂))))  
2. ¬Saw(Hazel,Hazel)  
3. ∀d (Donkey(d) → ¬Saw(d,Hazel))    (no donkey saw Hazel)  
4. ¬Human(Hazel) → ∀d (Donkey(d) → Saw(d,Hazel))  
   (“Hazel isn’t a human only if every donkey saw Hazel”)

Step 1.  Hazel must be a human.  
Assume, for contradiction, ¬Human(Hazel).  
Then by 4, every donkey sees Hazel; by 3, no donkey sees Hazel.  
Since at least one donkey exists, this is impossible.  
Hence Human(Hazel) is true.

Step 2.  What follows for donkeys?  
Because Hazel is a human and, by 3, no donkey sees Hazel, every donkey fails to
see at least one human.  Therefore

     ∀d (Donkey(d) → ¬∀h (Human(h) → Saw(d,h)))

which is exactly:

     No donkey saw every human.

So statement 3 is forced by the premises.

Step 3.  The other candidate statements are not forced.

• Statement 1 (Every donkey saw a human)  
 Make one donkey that sees nobody; all premises stay satisfied ⇒ 1 can be false.

• Statement 2 (No donkeys saw a human)  
 Add a second human that the donkey does see; premises stay satisfied ⇒ 2 can be false.

• Statement 4 (A donkey saw a human)  
 False in the first model above, true in the second ⇒ not forced.

• Statement 5 (Every donkey saw every human)  
 Contradicts premise 3 (they do not see Hazel) ⇒ always false, hence not forced true.

• Statement 6 (A donkey saw every human)  
 Also impossible because of Hazel, therefore not forced true.

Conclusion:  

Among the six listed statements, the only one that must be true is

3. No donkeys saw every human.

question.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a donkey, or a human, or a monkey (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. Lewis is a human. Every human chased every donkey that chased every monkey. A monkey is happy but it is not the case that Lewis is happy.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. No humans are happy.
2. Every human is happy.
3. A human is happy.
4. Only humans are happy.
5. A human is happy and every human is happy.
6. Not all humans are happy.

response.raw_response:
Let  

• Donkey(x), Human(x), Monkey(x) be mutually-exclusive unary predicates that exhaust the domain (and each sort is non-empty).  
• Lewis be a constant with Human(Lewis).  
• Chased(x,y) the binary “x chased y”.  
• Happy(x) the unary “x is happy”.

Given premises  

P1  ∀h( Human(h) → ∀d( Donkey(d) ∧ ∀m( Monkey(m) → Chased(d,m))  →  Chased(h,d) ) )  
      (Every human chased every donkey that chased every monkey)  

P2  ∃m( Monkey(m) ∧ Happy(m) )  (A monkey is happy)  

P3  ¬Happy(Lewis)  (Lewis is not happy)

No other premise mentions “happy”, so the only information we have about happiness is that  
• at least one monkey is happy, and  
• Lewis (a human) is not happy.

Now check each candidate statement to see whether it must hold in every model of P1–P3.

1. No humans are happy.  
   False in some models: we can add another human Harry with Happy(Harry). Premises remain satisfied. So it is not forced.

2. Every human is happy.  
   Impossible, because Lewis is a human and is not happy (P3). Hence the statement is not only unforced; it is outright false in every model.

3. A human is happy.  
   Not forced: in a model where Lewis is the only human, no human is happy. So the statement is not guaranteed.

4. Only humans are happy.  
   Contradicted by P2 (there exists a happy monkey). Not forced.

5. A human is happy and every human is happy.  
   Contradicted by P3. Not forced.

6. Not all humans are happy (¬∀x(Human(x) → Happy(x))).  
   Necessarily true: Lewis witnesses a human that is not happy, so in every model at least one human is unhappy.

Therefore the single statement that must be true is:

6. Not all humans are happy.

question.question:
Consider the following situation:

Everything is a donkey, or a human, or a monkey (exclusively), and there's at least one of each. Not all monkeys will run but it is not the case that Lewis didn't chase Hazel. Every monkey will run unless every donkey is happy. Alfred isn't a human unless no donkeys are happy. If every monkey chased Lewis, then not all donkeys saw Lewis. If every monkey chased Hazel, then Lewis is a donkey only if every human saw Hazel.

Which, if any, of the following statements must be true in this situation?

1. Every donkey is happy.
2. Not all donkeys will run and every donkey will run.
3. Not all donkeys are happy.
4. No donkeys are happy.
5. Only donkeys are happy.
6. Alfred will run.

response.raw_response:
First turn the English information into precise conditionals.

Sorts  
Every object is exactly one of: donkey (D), human (H) or monkey (M); each sort is non-empty.

Predicates  
Happy(x), Run(x), Chase(x,y), Saw(x,y) and the named individuals Alfred, Lewis, Hazel.

Premises  

P1  ¬∀m (M(m) → Run(m))                    (Not all monkeys will run.)  
    ⇔ ∃m (M(m) ∧ ¬Run(m)).                       

P2  Chase(Lewis, Hazel).                   (Lewis chased Hazel.)

P3  (¬∀d (D(d) → Happy(d))) → ∀m (M(m) → Run(m)).  
    (If some donkey is not happy then every monkey will run.)  

P4  (∃d (D(d) ∧ Happy(d))) → ¬H(Alfred).  
    (If at least one donkey is happy then Alfred is not a human.)  

P5  (∀m (M(m) → Chase(m, Lewis))) → ¬∀d (D(d) → Saw(d, Lewis)).  

P6  (∀m (M(m) → Chase(m, Hazel))) → (D(Lewis) → ∀h (H(h) → Saw(h, Hazel))).

Deriving what must be true

1.  From P1 we have some monkey m₀ with ¬Run(m₀).  
    Take the contrapositive of P3:

    (∃m (M(m) ∧ ¬Run(m))) → ∀d (D(d) → Happy(d)).

    Plugging m₀ in gives

    ∀d (D(d) → Happy(d)) ,

    i.e. every donkey is happy.  This conclusion depends only on the premises and is therefore necessary.

2.  Because at least one donkey exists, “every donkey is happy’’ entails “some donkey is happy’’.  
    So the antecedent of P4 is true, whence ¬H(Alfred); Alfred is either a donkey or a monkey.  
    Nothing in the premises forces Alfred to run, so his running is not necessary.

Nothing else follows inevitably from the premises.  In particular one can build a model in which

• all donkeys are happy (as shown above);  
• some monkey does not run (satisfying P1);  
• Alfred is a (happy) donkey who may or may not run;  
• the remaining predicates are assigned so that P2, P5 and P6 hold vacuously or by choice.

Thus, of the listed candidate conclusions only one is forced by the information given.

Must-true statements

1. Every donkey is happy.

All the other statements (2–6) can be false in some situation that satisfies the premises, so none of them is necessary.
